**(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the Shapley value of each player. Show all steps of the iteration over $S \subseteq N \setminus \{i\}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Verify whether the allocation $(\phi_1, \phi_2, \phi_3)$ lies in the **core** by checking every coalition $S \subseteq N$.

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Shapley value computation:**

By symmetry, focus on $\phi_1$. The coalitions not containing player 1 are: $\emptyset, \{2\}, \{3\}, \{2, 3\}$.

Marginal contributions $m_1(S) = v(S \cup \{1\}) - v(S)$:

| $S$ | $v(S \cup \{1\})$ | $v(S)$ | $m_1(S)$ | $\vert S \vert$ | Weight $\dfrac{\vert S \vert!\,(3 - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{3!}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\emptyset$ | $v(\{1\}) = 8$ | $0$ | $8$ | $0$ | $\dfrac{0! \cdot 2!}{6} = \dfrac{1}{3}$ |
| $\{2\}$ | $v(\{1,2\}) = 18$ | $8$ | $10$ | $1$ | $\dfrac{1! \cdot 1!}{6} = \dfrac{1}{6}$ |
| $\{3\}$ | $v(\{1,3\}) = 18$ | $8$ | $10$ | $1$ | $\dfrac{1! \cdot 1!}{6} = \dfrac{1}{6}$ |
| $\{2,3\}$ | $v(\{1,2,3\}) = 30$ | $18$ | $12$ | $2$ | $\dfrac{2! \cdot 0!}{6} = \dfrac{1}{3}$ |

Summing weighted contributions:

$$\phi_1 = \frac{1}{3}(8) + \frac{1}{6}(10) + \frac{1}{6}(10) + \frac{1}{3}(12) = \frac{8}{3} + \frac{10}{6} + \frac{10}{6} + 4 = \frac{8}{3} + \frac{10}{3} + 4 = 6 + 4 = 10$$

By symmetry of the characteristic function, $\phi_2 = \phi_3 = 10$. Sanity check: $\phi_1 + \phi_2 + \phi_3 = 30 = v(N)$ ✓.

**(b) Core verification:**

The core requires $\sum_{i \in S} \phi_i \geq v(S)$ for every $S \subseteq N$.

| $S$ | $\sum_{i \in S} \phi_i$ | $v(S)$ | Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\emptyset$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0 \geq 0$ ✓ |
| $\{1\}$ | $10$ | $8$ | $10 \geq 8$ ✓ |
| $\{2\}$ | $10$ | $8$ | $10 \geq 8$ ✓ |
| $\{3\}$ | $10$ | $8$ | $10 \geq 8$ ✓ |
| $\{1,2\}$ | $20$ | $18$ | $20 \geq 18$ ✓ |
| $\{1,3\}$ | $20$ | $18$ | $20 \geq 18$ ✓ |
| $\{2,3\}$ | $20$ | $18$ | $20 \geq 18$ ✓ |
| $\{1,2,3\}$ | $30$ | $30$ | $30 \geq 30$ ✓ |

All constraints are satisfied ⇒ the Shapley value $(10, 10, 10)$ lies **in the core** of this game. (Note: this is a special property of this symmetric, *concave* game — not true of every game.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of the cross-partial:** Many students confuse "complements" with "positive payoff". The correct test is the **cross-partial derivative** $\frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial s_i \partial s_j} > 0$, *not* the sign of $u_i$ itself. Lose 2 marks if you state the wrong test.
> 2. **Coalition complement ≠ grand coalition:** Students often confuse the "complement of $S$" with the "complement to the grand coalition", but $S \cup S^c = N$ already — there is nothing further to "add". The complement is the set $N \setminus S$, full stop.
> 3. **Shapley factorial weights:** A common mistake is using $\frac{1}{2^n}$ (which is wrong) instead of $\frac{\vert S \vert!\,(n - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{n!}$. Memorize the latter.
> 4. **Forgetting $\sum \phi_i = v(N)$:** If your Shapley values do not sum to $v(N)$, you have made a computational error. Always include the sanity check.
> 5. **Confusing complements with substitutes:** In a Cournot duopoly (linear inverse demand $a - Q$), the cross-partial is *negative* — that is a *substitutes* game, not a complements game. Examiners test this trap repeatedly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two meanings of "Complement"** in Module 2: **(1) Strategic complement** in non-cooperative games; **(2) Coalition complement** in cooperative games.
- **Strategic complement test:** $\frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial s_i \, \partial s_j} > 0$. Equivalent to best-response *increasing* in opponent's strategy.
- **Strategic substitute test:** $\frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial s_i \, \partial s_j} < 0$. Equivalent to best-response *decreasing* in opponent's strategy.
- **Complement of a coalition:** $S^c = N \setminus S$. Cardinality: $\vert S \vert + \vert S^c \vert = n$. Double complement: $(S^c)^c = S$. Union: $S \cup S^c = N$. Disjointness: $S \cap S^c = \emptyset$.
- **Supermodular game:** A game of strategic complements. By the **Tarski Fixed-Point Theorem**, pure-strategy Nash equilibria exist and form a *lattice* with a greatest and a least element.
- **Engineering applications of strategic complements:** **combinatorial auctions** (spectrum, electricity, advertising slots), **network goods**, **platform markets** (e.g., two-sided markets where buyer and seller participation reinforce each other).
- **Shapley value formula:** $\phi_i(v) = \sum_{S \subseteq N \setminus \{i\}} \frac{\vert S \vert!\,(n - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{n!}\,[v(S \cup \{i\}) - v(S)]$.
- **Core condition:** $\sum_{i \in S} x_i \geq v(S)$ for all $S \subseteq N$, with $\sum_{i \in N} x_i = v(N)$ for efficiency.
- **Cournot ≠ Complements:** Linear Cournot duopoly is a *substitutes* game. The complements duopoly requires a positive cross-term in the inverse demand (e.g., $P_1 = a - q_1 + \beta q_2$ with $\beta > 0$).
- **Always sanity-check:** Shapley values must sum to $v(N)$; best responses must be non-negative in economically meaningful problems; coalition complements must be subsets of $N$.
- **Real-world anchors to remember:** Spectrum auctions (complements), Cournot competition (substitutes), open-source project teams (coalition complements), Shapley's original airport cost-allocation example (Shapley value).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cartesian Product, Fuzzy Composition - Max-Min
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST753 - GAME THEORY AND MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cartesian Product, Fuzzy Composition - Max-Min

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cartesian Product of Fuzzy Sets

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
Let $\tilde{A}$ be a fuzzy set defined on universe of discourse $X$ and $\tilde{B}$ be a fuzzy set defined on universe of discourse $Y$. The **Cartesian product** of $\tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{B}$, denoted $\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}$, is a fuzzy relation defined on the Cartesian product space $X \times Y$ whose membership function is given by:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}}(x, y) = \min \left[ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(y) \right]$$

for every ordered pair $(x, y) \in X \times Y$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the KTU syllabus for fuzzy systems, the Cartesian product is the foundational building block for constructing **fuzzy relations** from individual fuzzy sets. Without it, max-min composition (covered in Section 1.2) cannot be performed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** The result of $\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}$ is **NOT a fuzzy set** — it is a **fuzzy relation** (degree of association between elements of two universes). The output universe becomes $X \times Y$, not a single universe.

### 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine two fuzzy sets describing the same person from different perspectives:
- $\tilde{A}$ = "Tallness" on the universe $X = \{160\text{cm}, 170\text{cm}, 180\text{cm}\}$
- $\tilde{B}$ = "Heaviness" on the universe $Y = \{60\text{kg}, 70\text{kg}, 80\text{kg}\}$

The Cartesian product $\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}$ gives you a **graded compatibility chart** — a 2D table telling you, for every (height, weight) pair, how plausible that combination is for your "ideal tall and heavy person" profile. Each cell holds a value in $[0,1]$, where the **minimum operator** ensures that if either dimension fails (low membership in $\tilde{A}$ or $\tilde{B}$), the combined score is dragged down. This mirrors real life: a person who is tall but not heavy scores lower than one who is moderately tall AND moderately heavy.

### 1.3 Fuzzy Composition — Max-Min Composition

**Formal Definition:**
Let $\tilde{R}$ be a fuzzy relation on $X \times Y$ and $\tilde{S}$ be a fuzzy relation on $Y \times Z$. The **max-min composition** of $\tilde{R}$ and $\tilde{S}$, denoted $\tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R}$, is a fuzzy relation on $X \times Z$ defined as:

$$\mu_{\tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R}}(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} \left[ \min \left( \mu_{\tilde{R}}(x, y), \mu_{\tilde{S}}(y, z) \right) \right]$$

for every $(x, z) \in X \times Z$.

### 1.4 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Picture a fuzzy **inference chain**:
- $\tilde{R}$ tells you: "How much does $x$ belong to $y$?"
- $\tilde{S}$ tells you: "How much does $y$ imply $z$?"
- $\tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R}$ asks: "**What is the strongest possible indirect link from $x$ to $z$, passing through some intermediate $y$?**"

The **min** picks the bottleneck (weakest link) along a single path through a chosen intermediate $y$. The **max** then sweeps over all possible intermediates and keeps the best path — exactly like selecting the **strongest chain of reasoning** in an expert system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Membership values are **dimensionless** real numbers in the closed interval $[0, 1]$, where $0$ indicates no membership and $1$ indicates full membership.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why the Min Operator in Cartesian Product?

The min operator $\min(a, b)$ is used in the Cartesian product because it satisfies the properties required for a **t-norm** (triangular norm):
1. **Commutativity**: $\min(a, b) = \min(b, a)$
2. **Associativity**: $\min(\min(a, b), c) = \min(a, \min(b, c))$
3. **Monotonicity**: If $a \leq c$ and $b \leq d$, then $\min(a, b) \leq \min(c, d)$
4. **Boundary conditions**: $\min(a, 1) = a$ and $\min(a, 0) = 0$

These guarantee that the resulting fuzzy relation behaves consistently under scaling and combination.

### 2.2 Why Max-Min in Composition?

The composition uses **min** as the t-norm (for path aggregation) and **max** as the t-conorm (for path selection):
- **Min** = conservative aggregation along a path (worst-case bottleneck logic)
- **Max** = optimistic selection across paths (best-case propagation logic)

This duality mimics human decision-making under uncertainty — choose the best achievable intermediate while respecting each path's weakest link.

### 2.3 Operational Logic — Step by Step

For computing $\tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R}$ for a single cell $(x, z)$:
1. **Pick a row** $x$ in $\tilde{R}$.
2. **Pick a column** $y$ in $\tilde{R}$ — this gives you $\mu_{\tilde{R}}(x, y)$.
3. **Use the same $y$ to pick a row** in $\tilde{S}$ — this gives you $\mu_{\tilde{S}}(y, z)$.
4. **Compute the min** of these two values: $\min(\mu_{\tilde{R}}(x, y), \mu_{\tilde{S}}(y, z))$.
5. **Repeat** for every $y \in Y$.
6. **Take the max** of all the min values.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Universe / Domain | Result Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian Product | $\mu_{\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}}(x, y) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(y))$ | $X \times Y$ | Fuzzy Relation |
| Max-Min Composition | $\mu_{\tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R}}(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} \left[ \min(\mu_{\tilde{R}}(x, y), \mu_{\tilde{S}}(y, z)) \right]$ | $X \times Z$ | Fuzzy Relation |
| Range of Membership | $0 \leq \mu \leq 1$ | All fuzzy values | Dimensionless |
| Min Operator Property | $\min(a, b) \leq a$ and $\min(a, b) \leq b$ | Path aggregation | Weakest link |
| Max Operator Property | $\max(a, b) \geq a$ and $\max(a, b) \geq b$ | Path selection | Best link |
| Composition Dimension | $\vert \tilde{R} \vert = m \times n$, $\vert \tilde{S} \vert = n \times p$ $\Rightarrow$ $\vert \tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R} \vert = m \times p$ | Matrix dimensions | Compatibility |
| Empty Path Rule | If $\min(\cdot, \cdot) = 0$ for all $y$, then $\mu_{\tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R}}(x, z) = 0$ | No linking intermediate | Zero membership |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Expert Systems & Medical Diagnosis:** Linking symptom $\to$ disease $\to$ treatment using fuzzy chains.
- **Fuzzy Controllers (e.g., Washing Machines, Air Conditioners):** Mapping sensor inputs $\to$ rule antecedents $\to$ actuator outputs.
- **Pattern Recognition:** Composing similarity relations for handwriting or image classification.
- **Decision Support Systems:** Multi-criteria decision analysis under linguistic vagueness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cartesian Product Calculation

**Problem:** Given fuzzy sets
$\tilde{A} = \{0.2/x_1, 0.5/x_2, 0.8/x_3\}$ on $X = \{x_1, x_2, x_3\}$
$\tilde{B} = \{0.6/y_1, 0.9/y_2\}$ on $Y = \{y_1, y_2\}$

Compute $\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}$.

**Step 1 — Set up the relational matrix structure.**

The Cartesian product matrix will be of size $3 \times 2$ (rows = $X$ elements, columns = $Y$ elements).

**Step 2 — Apply the formula $\mu_{\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}}(x, y) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(y))$.**

For cell $(x_1, y_1)$:
$$\mu(x_1, y_1) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_1), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(y_1)) = \min(0.2, 0.6) = 0.2$$

For cell $(x_1, y_2)$:
$$\mu(x_1, y_2) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_1), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(y_2)) = \min(0.2, 0.9) = 0.2$$

For cell $(x_2, y_1)$:
$$\mu(x_2, y_1) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_2), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(y_1)) = \min(0.5, 0.6) = 0.5$$

For cell $(x_2, y_2)$:
$$\mu(x_2, y_2) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_2), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(y_2)) = \min(0.5, 0.9) = 0.5$$

For cell $(x_3, y_1)$:
$$\mu(x_3, y_1) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_3), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(y_1)) = \min(0.8, 0.6) = 0.6$$

For cell $(x_3, y_2)$:
$$\mu(x_3, y_2) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_3), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(y_2)) = \min(0.8, 0.9) = 0.8$$

**Step 3 — Assemble the resulting relation matrix.**

$$\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.2 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.6 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

where the $(i,j)$-th entry is $\mu_{\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}}(x_i, y_j)$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Max-Min Composition

**Problem:** Given fuzzy relations
$\tilde{R}$ on $X \times Y$ (size $2 \times 3$) and $\tilde{S}$ on $Y \times Z$ (size $3 \times 2$).

$$\tilde{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.9 & 0.3 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \tilde{S} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.2 \\ 0.5 & 0.8 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute $\tilde{T} = \tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R}$ on $X \times Z$ (size $2 \times 2$).

**Step 1 — Determine dimensions.**
$X = \{x_1, x_2\}$, $Y = \{y_1, y_2, y_3\}$, $Z = \{z_1, z_2\}$. Result $\tilde{T}$ is $2 \times 2$.

**Step 2 — Compute entry $\tilde{T}(x_1, z_1)$ using max-min formula.**

$$\tilde{T}(x_1, z_1) = \max_{y \in \{y_1, y_2, y_3\}} \left[ \min(\tilde{R}(x_1, y), \tilde{S}(y, z_1)) \right]$$

Sub-step 2a — For $y = y_1$:
$$\min(\tilde{R}(x_1, y_1), \tilde{S}(y_1, z_1)) = \min(0.1, 0.7) = 0.1$$

Sub-step 2b — For $y = y_2$:
$$\min(\tilde{R}(x_1, y_2), \tilde{S}(y_2, z_1)) = \min(0.6, 0.5) = 0.5$$

Sub-step 2c — For $y = y_3$:
$$\min(\tilde{R}(x_1, y_3), \tilde{S}(y_3, z_1)) = \min(0.4, 0.4) = 0.4$$

Sub-step 2d — Take the max:
$$\tilde{T}(x_1, z_1) = \max(0.1, 0.5, 0.4) = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Compute entry $\tilde{T}(x_1, z_2)$.**

Sub-step 3a — For $y = y_1$:
$$\min(\tilde{R}(x_1, y_1), \tilde{S}(y_1, z_2)) = \min(0.1, 0.2) = 0.1$$

Sub-step 3b — For $y = y_2$:
$$\min(\tilde{R}(x_1, y_2), \tilde{S}(y_2, z_2)) = \min(0.6, 0.8) = 0.6$$

Sub-step 3c — For $y = y_3$:
$$\min(\tilde{R}(x_1, y_3), \tilde{S}(y_3, z_2)) = \min(0.4, 0.6) = 0.4$$

Sub-step 3d — Take the max:
$$\tilde{T}(x_1, z_2) = \max(0.1, 0.6, 0.4) = 0.6$$

**Step 4 — Compute entry $\tilde{T}(x_2, z_1)$.**

Sub-step 4a — For $y = y_1$:
$$\min(\tilde{R}(x_2, y_1), \tilde{S}(y_1, z_1)) = \min(0.9, 0.7) = 0.7$$

Sub-step 4b — For $y = y_2$:
$$\min(\tilde{R}(x_2, y_2), \tilde{S}(y_2, z_1)) = \min(0.3, 0.5) = 0.3$$

Sub-step 4c — For $y = y_3$:
$$\min(\tilde{R}(x_2, y_3), \tilde{S}(y_3, z_1)) = \min(0.5, 0.4) = 0.4$$

Sub-step 4d — Take the max:
$$\tilde{T}(x_2, z_1) = \max(0.7, 0.3, 0.4) = 0.7$$

**Step 5 — Compute entry $\tilde{T}(x_2, z_2)$.**

Sub-step 5a — For $y = y_1$:
$$\min(\tilde{R}(x_2, y_1), \tilde{S}(y_1, z_2)) = \min(0.9, 0.2) = 0.2$$

Sub-step 5b — For $y = y_2$:
$$\min(\tilde{R}(x_2, y_2), \tilde{S}(y_2, z_2)) = \min(0.3, 0.8) = 0.3$$

Sub-step 5c — For $y = y_3$:
$$\min(\tilde{R}(x_2, y_3), \tilde{S}(y_3, z_2)) = \min(0.5, 0.6) = 0.5$$

Sub-step 5d — Take the max:
$$\tilde{T}(x_2, z_2) = \max(0.2, 0.3, 0.5) = 0.5$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the final composition matrix.**

$$\tilde{T} = \tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.6 \\ 0.7 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Verification via Matrix Dimension Compatibility Check.**

Since $\tilde{R}$ is $2 \times 3$ and $\tilde{S}$ is $3 \times 2$, the inner dimension ($3$) matches. The result $\tilde{T}$ correctly has outer dimensions $2 \times 2$. ✓

---

### 3.3 Analytical Quick-Reference Matrix

| Operation | Input 1 Type | Input 2 Type | Operator Inside | Operator Across | Output Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian Product | Fuzzy Set | Fuzzy Set | $\min$ | None | Fuzzy Relation |
| Max-Min Composition | Fuzzy Relation | Fuzzy Relation | $\min$ | $\max$ | Fuzzy Relation |
| Max-Product Composition | Fuzzy Relation | Fuzzy Relation | $\cdot$ (product) | $\max$ | Fuzzy Relation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fuzzy Composition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX["Universe X"] --> matR["Fuzzy Relation R Matrix m x n"]
    nodeY["Universe Y"] --> matR
    matR --> compMin["Min Operator Path Aggregation"]
    nodeY --> matS["Fuzzy Relation S Matrix n x p"]
    nodeZ["Universe Z"] --> matS
    matS --> compMin
    compMin --> compMax["Max Operator Path Selection"]
    compMax --> matT["Fuzzy Relation T Matrix m x p"]
    nodeX --> matT
    nodeZ --> matT
    matT --> output["Resulting Fuzzy Relation T equals S composed with R"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cartesian Product Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    setA["Fuzzy Set A on Universe X"] --> takeMuA["Extract Membership muA of x"]
    setB["Fuzzy Set B on Universe Y"] --> takeMuB["Extract Membership muB of y"]
    takeMuA --> minOp["Apply Min Operator"]
    takeMuB --> minOp
    minOp --> relAB["Fuzzy Relation A cross B on X cross Y"]
    relAB --> note["Each cell holds min value between 0 and 1"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Composition Computation per Cell

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as Solver
    participant R as Relation R
    participant S as Relation S
    participant T as Relation T
    U->>R: Pick row x and column y
    R-->>U: Return muR of x comma y
    U->>S: Use same y, pick column z
    S-->>U: Return muS of y comma z
    U->>U: Compute min of muR and muS
    U->>R: Iterate over all y values
    R-->>U: Return remaining muR values
    U->>U: Compute max over all min values
    U->>T: Store result at position x comma z
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inference Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["Input Crisp x"] --> fuzz["Fuzzification"]
    fuzz --> relR["Apply Relation R x to y"]
    relR --> comp["Max Min Composition"]
    relS["Relation S y to z"] --> comp
    comp --> defuzz["Defuzzification optional"]
    defuzz --> outC["Crisp Output z"]
    defuzz --> outF["Fuzzy Output z if retained"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**
*Define the Cartesian product of two fuzzy sets. State the operator used and the type of result produced.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Cartesian product of two fuzzy sets $\tilde{A}$ on universe $X$ and $\tilde{B}$ on universe $Y$ is a fuzzy relation on $X \times Y$ defined as:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}}(x, y) = \min\left[\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(y)\right]$$

The operator used is the **minimum (min)** operator. The result produced is a **fuzzy relation**, not a fuzzy set, because the resulting structure captures the degree of association between elements of two distinct universes. **(3 Marks — 1 for definition, 1 for operator, 1 for result type)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**
*What is max-min composition? Write its mathematical definition.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Max-min composition is a method of combining two fuzzy relations $\tilde{R}$ (on $X \times Y$) and $\tilde{S}$ (on $Y \times Z$) to derive a new fuzzy relation $\tilde{T} = \tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R}$ on $X \times Z$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R}}(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} \left[ \min\left(\mu_{\tilde{R}}(x, y), \mu_{\tilde{S}}(y, z)\right) \right]$$

It uses the **min** operator as a t-norm for aggregating along a single path through an intermediate $y$, and the **max** operator as a t-conorm for selecting the best path across all intermediates. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Past Pattern]**

(a) *Given two fuzzy sets:*
$\tilde{A} = \{0.3/x_1, 0.7/x_2, 1.0/x_3, 0.5/x_4\}$ on $X = \{x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4\}$
$\tilde{B} = \{0.4/y_1, 0.8/y_2, 0.6/y_3\}$ on $Y = \{y_1, y_2, y_3\}$

*Compute the Cartesian product $\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}$. Show all steps.* **(7 Marks)**

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify universe sizes.**
$X$ has $4$ elements, $Y$ has $3$ elements. The Cartesian product matrix will be $4 \times 3$.

**Step 2 — Apply $\mu_{\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}}(x, y) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(y))$ to each cell.**

Row $x_1$: $\min(0.3, 0.4) = 0.3$, $\min(0.3, 0.8) = 0.3$, $\min(0.3, 0.6) = 0.3$
Row $x_2$: $\min(0.7, 0.4) = 0.4$, $\min(0.7, 0.8) = 0.7$, $\min(0.7, 0.6) = 0.6$
Row $x_3$: $\min(1.0, 0.4) = 0.4$, $\min(1.0, 0.8) = 0.8$, $\min(1.0, 0.6) = 0.6$
Row $x_4$: $\min(0.5, 0.4) = 0.4$, $\min(0.5, 0.8) = 0.5$, $\min(0.5, 0.6) = 0.5$

**Step 3 — Result Matrix:**

$$\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.3 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.7 & 0.6 \\ 0.4 & 0.8 & 0.6 \\ 0.4 & 0.5 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(7 Marks — 1 for dimension identification, 4 for all 12 cells, 1 for matrix assembly, 1 for result type statement)**

---

(b) *Given two fuzzy relations:*

$$\tilde{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.5 & 0.9 \\ 0.6 & 0.4 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \tilde{S} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.3 \\ 0.5 & 0.7 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

*Compute the max-min composition $\tilde{T} = \tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R}$. Show all intermediate steps.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Dimension check.** $\tilde{R}$ is $2 \times 3$, $\tilde{S}$ is $3 \times 2$. Inner dimension matches ($3 = 3$). Result $\tilde{T}$ is $2 \times 2$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 2 — Compute $\tilde{T}(x_1, z_1)$:**
$$\min(0.2, 0.8) = 0.2, \quad \min(0.5, 0.5) = 0.5, \quad \min(0.9, 0.2) = 0.2$$
$$\tilde{T}(x_1, z_1) = \max(0.2, 0.5, 0.2) = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\tilde{T}(x_1, z_2)$:**
$$\min(0.2, 0.3) = 0.2, \quad \min(0.5, 0.7) = 0.5, \quad \min(0.9, 0.6) = 0.6$$
$$\tilde{T}(x_1, z_2) = \max(0.2, 0.5, 0.6) = 0.6$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\tilde{T}(x_2, z_1)$:**
$$\min(0.6, 0.8) = 0.6, \quad \min(0.4, 0.5) = 0.4, \quad \min(0.1, 0.2) = 0.1$$
$$\tilde{T}(x_2, z_1) = \max(0.6, 0.4, 0.1) = 0.6$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\tilde{T}(x_2, z_2)$:**
$$\min(0.6, 0.3) = 0.3, \quad \min(0.4, 0.7) = 0.4, \quad \min(0.1, 0.6) = 0.1$$
$$\tilde{T}(x_2, z_2) = \max(0.3, 0.4, 0.1) = 0.4$$

**Step 6 — Final Matrix:**

$$\tilde{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.6 \\ 0.6 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(7 Marks — 1 for dimension check, 4 for all 4 cells with intermediates, 1 for matrix, 1 for verification note)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Explain the concept of fuzzy Cartesian product with a suitable example. Discuss why the min operator is preferred over the product operator for this construction.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The fuzzy Cartesian product generalizes the classical set Cartesian product to handle graded memberships. For fuzzy sets $\tilde{A}$ on $X$ and $\tilde{B}$ on $Y$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}}(x, y) = \min\left[\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(y)\right]$$

**Example:** Consider $\tilde{A} = \text{"Experienced"}$ on engineers with $\mu = \{0.4, 0.7, 0.9\}$ and $\tilde{B} = \text{"Skilled"}$ on programmers with $\mu = \{0.5, 0.8\}$. The product gives a relation showing how well an engineer-programmer pair matches both criteria simultaneously. **(2 Marks for concept, 2 Marks for example)**

**Why min over product?**
- Min satisfies all t-norm properties (commutativity, associativity, monotonicity, boundary conditions). **(1 Mark)**
- Min produces results in the same membership range $[0,1]$ without inflation — product can yield values close to 0 even when both inputs are moderate. **(1 Mark)**
- Min is computationally simpler and preserves the **weakest-link semantics**: a pair is only as plausible as its weaker dimension. **(1 Mark)**

**(7 Marks Total)**

---

**(b)** *Given fuzzy relations:*

$$\tilde{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.2 & 0.7 \\ 0.9 & 0.5 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \tilde{S} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.8 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix}$$

*Compute $\tilde{T} = \tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R}$ and verify the result lies in $[0,1]$.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Dimensions:** $\tilde{R}$ is $2 \times 3$, $\tilde{S}$ is $3 \times 2$. Result $\tilde{T}$ is $2 \times 2$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 2 — $\tilde{T}(x_1, z_1)$:** $\min(0.4,0.6)=0.4$, $\min(0.2,0.8)=0.2$, $\min(0.7,0.1)=0.1$. Max = $0.4$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 3 — $\tilde{T}(x_1, z_2)$:** $\min(0.4,0.4)=0.4$, $\min(0.2,0.2)=0.2$, $\min(0.7,0.9)=0.7$. Max = $0.7$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 4 — $\tilde{T}(x_2, z_1)$:** $\min(0.9,0.6)=0.6$, $\min(0.5,0.8)=0.5$, $\min(0.3,0.1)=0.1$. Max = $0.6$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 5 — $\tilde{T}(x_2, z_2)$:** $\min(0.9,0.4)=0.4$, $\min(0.5,0.2)=0.2$, $\min(0.3,0.9)=0.3$. Max = $0.4$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 6 — Final Matrix and Verification:**

$$\tilde{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.7 \\ 0.6 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

All entries satisfy $0 \leq \mu \leq 1$. ✓ **(2 Marks)**

**(7 Marks Total)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Reversing $\tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R}$ order** — composition is **not commutative**; the inner dimension mismatch is the most common $-2$ mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing fuzzy SET with fuzzy RELATION** — stating "Cartesian product gives a fuzzy set" costs $-1$ mark; it gives a fuzzy **relation**.
> 3. **Skipping intermediate min values** in composition — examiners expect all three min computations shown per cell before the max; skipping earns partial credit only.
> 4. **Using product instead of min** — students often substitute $\mu_A(x) \cdot \mu_B(y)$ by mistake; this is **max-product composition**, a different topic.
> 5. **Forgetting the result universe** — always state that $\tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R}$ lives on $X \times Z$, not on $Y$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Cartesian product** uses the **min** operator and yields a **fuzzy relation** on $X \times Y$.
- ✅ **Max-min composition** uses **min along the path** and **max across paths** to chain two fuzzy relations.
- ✅ The result of $\tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R}$ is a fuzzy relation on $X \times Z$; matrix dimensions must satisfy $m \times n$ multiplied with $n \times p$ giving $m \times p$.
- ✅ Membership values are **always** in the closed interval $[0,1]$; no negative or values greater than $1$ are valid.
- ✅ Composition is **not commutative**: $\tilde{S} \circ \tilde{R} \neq \tilde{R} \circ \tilde{S}$ in general.
- ✅ The **min** operator acts as the bottleneck (weakest link) per path; the **max** operator picks the strongest available path.
- ✅ For an $m \times n$ relation $\tilde{R}$ and an $n \times p$ relation $\tilde{S}$, the composition requires exactly $m \times p \times n$ elementary min-max operations.
- ✅ **Common engineering applications:** fuzzy expert systems, fuzzy controllers, decision support systems, and pattern recognition pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Max-Product, Extension Principle, Fuzzy Arithmetic - Fuzzy Numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST753 - GAME THEORY AND MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Max-Product, Extension Principle, Fuzzy Arithmetic - Fuzzy Numbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Max-Product Composition

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
The **Max-Product Composition** is a binary operation on two fuzzy relations $R \subseteq X \times Y$ and $S \subseteq Y \times Z$, denoted by $R \circ_p S$, defined as:

$$(R \circ_p S)(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} \left[ \mu_R(x, y) \cdot \mu_S(y, z) \right]$$

where $\cdot$ represents ordinary multiplication, and the result $T = R \circ_p S$ is a fuzzy relation on $X \times Z$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** Max-Product is the algebraic product variant of composition. It is distinct from the **Max-Min Composition** $(R \circ S)(x,z) = \max_y \min[\mu_R(x,y), \mu_S(y,z)]$. Both are tested frequently in KTU 2024 Scheme questions.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are a logistics manager choosing a delivery route. Each origin-destination link has a "smoothness score" (between 0 and 1) and each destination-destination link has a "reliability score." 
- **Max-Min** asks: *What is the weakest link in the best path?* (it picks the minimum score on the path).
- **Max-Product** asks: *What is the combined strength of the best path?* (it multiplies the scores along the path, rewarding strong chains).

The "max" then selects the **best possible chain** of connections from origin to final destination. The result tells you the **overall strength of the optimal supply chain**.

### 1.2 Extension Principle (Zadeh's Extension Principle)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Extension Principle**, introduced by **Lotfi A. Zadeh** in 1975, is the cornerstone of fuzzy set theory. It provides a systematic method to extend any crisp (non-fuzzy) mathematical function $f : X \to Y$ to operate on fuzzy sets.

Given a fuzzy set $A$ in $X$ with membership function $\mu_A(x)$, the image $B = f(A)$ is a fuzzy set in $Y$ defined by:

$$\mu_B(y) = \begin{cases} \sup\limits_{x \in f^{-1}(y)} \mu_A(x), & \text{if } f^{-1}(y) \neq \emptyset \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Multi-Variable Extension:** For $f : X_1 \times X_2 \times \cdots \times X_n \to Y$ and fuzzy sets $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$:

$$\mu_B(y) = \sup_{(x_1,\dots,x_n) \in f^{-1}(y)} \min[\mu_{A_1}(x_1), \dots, \mu_{A_n}(x_n)]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** If you have a vague input (e.g., "around 5") and a precise function (e.g., $f(x) = x^2$), the Extension Principle gives you a principled way to compute the vague output ("around 25") rather than picking one crisp value.

### 1.3 Fuzzy Numbers

**Formal Definition:**
A **Fuzzy Number** $A$ is a fuzzy set on the real line $\mathbb{R}$ satisfying:
1. $A$ is **normal**: there exists $x_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ with $\mu_A(x_0) = 1$ (the *core*).
2. $A$ is **convex**: $\mu_A(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda)x_2) \geq \min[\mu_A(x_1), \mu_A(x_2)]$ for all $\lambda \in [0,1]$.
3. $\mu_A$ is **upper semi-continuous**.
4. The **support** $\{x : \mu_A(x) > 0\}$ is **bounded**, i.e., $A$ is compact.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
A fuzzy number is a "vague real number." Just as classical numbers like 5 or 10.7 are precise, fuzzy numbers represent imprecise quantities such as:
- "Approximately 50" (Triangular Fuzzy Number)
- "Somewhere between 40 and 60" (Trapezoidal Fuzzy Number)
- "Close to 100, with symmetric uncertainty" (Gaussian Fuzzy Number)

In **engineering practice**, fuzzy numbers model measurement uncertainty, expert estimates, sensor noise, and linguistic variables used in fuzzy controllers.

**Standard Types (Highlighted in KTU Syllabus):**

| Type | Notation | Core Property |
|------|----------|---------------|
| Triangular Fuzzy Number (TFN) | $A = (a, b, c)$ | Single peak at $b$ |
| Trapezoidal Fuzzy Number (TrFN) | $A = (a, b, c, d)$ | Flat top $[b,c]$ |
| Gaussian Fuzzy Number | $A = (m, \sigma)$ | Infinite support |
| LR Fuzzy Number | $A = (m, \alpha, \beta)_{LR}$ | Asymmetric reference functions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The course specifically tests **α-cuts** of fuzzy numbers. The α-cut of a fuzzy number $A$ is the crisp interval:
> $$[A]_\alpha = \{x \in \mathbb{R} : \mu_A(x) \geq \alpha\}, \quad \alpha \in (0,1]$$
> α-cuts transform fuzzy arithmetic into **interval arithmetic** — the bridge between fuzzy and classical math.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Max-Product Composition — Deep Analysis

**Operational Steps (KTU Board Pattern):**

1. **Identify Relations:** Confirm $R$ on $X \times Y$ and $S$ on $Y \times Z$ with shared index $Y$.
2. **Element-wise Product:** For each $(x,y,z)$ triple, compute the product $\mu_R(x,y) \cdot \mu_S(y,z)$.
3. **Maximize over $Y$:** For each fixed $(x, z)$, find the maximum product across all $y \in Y$.
4. **Construct $T(x,z)$:** Form the resulting fuzzy relation matrix.

**Why Multiplication (not Minimum)?**
The product operator is **strictly decreasing** in each argument and **fully annihilating** (any zero factor makes the product zero). This means Max-Product:
- **Penalizes** weak links more severely than Max-Min.
- **Rewards** consistently strong chains.
- Is **distributive** over the max operator, enabling clean algebraic manipulation.

**Algebraic Properties (Quick Reference):**
- **Commutative?** No (in general, since $R \circ_p S \neq S \circ_p R$ unless all relations are symmetric).
- **Associative?** Yes, $(R \circ_p S) \circ_p T = R \circ_p (S \circ_p T)$.
- **Distributive over union?** Yes, $R \circ_p (S \cup T) = (R \circ_p S) \cup (R \circ_p T)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Max-Product composition underlies **Fuzzy Cognitive Maps (FCMs)** used in decision-support systems, medical diagnosis, and policy analysis where the strength of causal chains matters multiplicatively.

### 2.2 Extension Principle — Deep Analysis

**The "Why" Behind the Supremum:**

The Extension Principle answers a fundamental question: *"If a precise function takes a vague input, what is the most we can say about the vague output?"*

For each output value $y \in Y$:
- Look at **all** inputs $x$ that map to $y$ via $f$ (the pre-image $f^{-1}(y)$).
- Among these, find the one with the **highest membership** in $A$.
- Assign that highest membership to $y$.

The **supremum** (least upper bound) is used because:
- If $f^{-1}(y)$ is empty, the output is impossible ($\mu_B(y) = 0$).
- If multiple inputs share the same output, take the **strongest** available input's degree of belonging.

### 2.3 Fuzzy Arithmetic — Deep Analysis

**Two Approaches to Fuzzy Arithmetic:**

**Approach A: α-Cut + Interval Arithmetic (Most Common in KTU)**
- Decompose each fuzzy number into nested α-cuts (which are crisp intervals).
- Apply standard interval arithmetic at each α level.
- Assemble results back into a fuzzy set.

**Approach B: Extension Principle Direct Computation**
- Apply the Extension Principle directly to arithmetic operations.
- For addition of fuzzy numbers $A$ and $B$:
  $$\mu_{A+B}(z) = \sup_{x+y=z} \min[\mu_A(x), \mu_B(y)]$$

**Key Theorem (α-Cut Equivalence):**
$$[A \oplus B]_\alpha = [A]_\alpha \oplus [B]_\alpha$$
where $\oplus$ denotes the corresponding interval operation. This theorem is the **backbone** of fuzzy arithmetic.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PRINT THIS TABLE — KTU 2024 Board Examiners reference these formulas directly.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Max-Product Composition | $(R \circ_p S)(x,z) = \max_y [\mu_R(x,y) \cdot \mu_S(y,z)]$ | $R \subseteq X \times Y$, $S \subseteq Y \times Z$ |
| 2 | Max-Min Composition | $(R \circ S)(x,z) = \max_y \min[\mu_R(x,y), \mu_S(y,z)]$ | Comparison baseline |
| 3 | Extension Principle (Single) | $\mu_B(y) = \sup_{x \in f^{-1}(y)} \mu_A(x)$ | $B = f(A)$ |
| 4 | Extension Principle (Multi) | $\mu_B(y) = \sup \min[\mu_{A_1}(x_1), \dots, \mu_{A_n}(x_n)]$ | Over $f^{-1}(y)$ |
| 5 | α-cut | $[A]_\alpha = \{x : \mu_A(x) \geq \alpha\}$ | $\alpha \in (0,1]$ |
| 6 | Triangular Fuzzy Number | $\mu_A(x) = (x-a)/(b-a)$ for $a \leq x \leq b$; $(c-x)/(c-b)$ for $b \leq x \leq c$ | TFN = $(a, b, c)$ |
| 7 | Trapezoidal Fuzzy Number | $\mu_A(x) = (x-a)/(b-a)$ for $a \leq x \leq b$; $1$ for $b \leq x \leq c$; $(d-x)/(d-c)$ for $c \leq x \leq d$ | TrFN = $(a, b, c, d)$ |
| 8 | Gaussian Fuzzy Number | $\mu_A(x) = \exp(-(x-m)^2 / (2\sigma^2))$ | $m$ = mean, $\sigma$ = spread |
| 9 | Addition of Fuzzy Numbers | $\mu_{A+B}(z) = \sup_{x+y=z} \min[\mu_A(x), \mu_B(y)]$ | Equivalent: $[A+B]_\alpha = [A]_\alpha + [B]_\alpha$ |
| 10 | Subtraction | $\mu_{A-B}(z) = \sup_{x-y=z} \min[\mu_A(x), \mu_B(y)]$ | $[A-B]_\alpha = [A]_\alpha - [B]_\alpha$ |
| 11 | Multiplication | $\mu_{A \cdot B}(z) = \sup_{x \cdot y=z} \min[\mu_A(x), \mu_B(y)]$ | $[A \cdot B]_\alpha = [A]_\alpha \cdot [B]_\alpha$ |
| 12 | Division | $\mu_{A/B}(z) = \sup_{x/y=z} \min[\mu_A(x), \mu_B(y)]$ | Requires $0 \notin [B]_\alpha$ for all $\alpha$ |
| 13 | Scalar Multiplication | $\mu_{kA}(x) = \mu_A(x/k)$ for $k > 0$ | $k$ real, $k \neq 0$ |
| 14 | α-cut Interval Sum | If $[A]_\alpha = [a_1, a_2]$, $[B]_\alpha = [b_1, b_2]$, then $[A+B]_\alpha = [a_1+b_1, a_2+b_2]$ | Interval arithmetic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of Fuzzy Arithmetic:** Used in **engineering risk analysis** (e.g., dam safety, structural loads), **fuzzy controllers** in washing machines and air conditioners, **economics** (vague forecasts), and **AI decision systems** where inputs are inherently uncertain.

### 2.5 Properties of Fuzzy Numbers (Why They Matter)

| Property | Statement | Engineering Meaning |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Convexity** | All α-cuts are intervals | Stable, well-defined uncertainty |
| **Normality** | $\exists x$ with $\mu(x) = 1$ | A "most representative" value exists |
| **Compact Support** | Bounded support | Computationally tractable |
| **Closure under addition** | $A + B$ is a fuzzy number | Arithmetic stability |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Max-Product Composition (Full Step-by-Step)

**Problem:** Let $X = \{x_1, x_2\}$, $Y = \{y_1, y_2, y_3\}$, $Z = \{z_1, z_2\}$. Given fuzzy relations:

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.7 & 0.5 \\ 0.9 & 0.3 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}_{2 \times 3} \quad S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.1 \\ 0.8 & 0.5 \\ 0.3 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix}_{3 \times 2}$$

Compute $T = R \circ_p S$ using **Max-Product composition**.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Identify the shared index.**
$R$ has shape $2 \times 3$ and $S$ has shape $3 \times 2$. The shared index is $Y$ (size 3). The result $T$ will have shape $2 \times 2$.

**Step 2: Compute $T(x_1, z_1)$.**
For fixed $(x_1, z_1)$, iterate over $y \in \{y_1, y_2, y_3\}$:

$$T(x_1, z_1) = \max_y \left[ R(x_1, y) \cdot S(y, z_1) \right]$$

Calculate each product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1: \quad & R(x_1, y_1) \cdot S(y_1, z_1) = 0.2 \times 0.6 = 0.12 \\
y_2: \quad & R(x_1, y_2) \cdot S(y_2, z_1) = 0.7 \times 0.8 = 0.56 \\
y_3: \quad & R(x_1, y_3) \cdot S(y_3, z_1) = 0.5 \times 0.3 = 0.15
\end{aligned}
$$

Take the maximum:

$$T(x_1, z_1) = \max\{0.12, 0.56, 0.15\} = 0.56$$

**Step 3: Compute $T(x_1, z_2)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1: \quad & 0.2 \times 0.1 = 0.02 \\
y_2: \quad & 0.7 \times 0.5 = 0.35 \\
y_3: \quad & 0.5 \times 0.9 = 0.45
\end{aligned}
$$

Maximum: $T(x_1, z_2) = \max\{0.02, 0.35, 0.45\} = 0.45$

**Step 4: Compute $T(x_2, z_1)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1: \quad & 0.9 \times 0.6 = 0.54 \\
y_2: \quad & 0.3 \times 0.8 = 0.24 \\
y_3: \quad & 0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.12
\end{aligned}
$$

Maximum: $T(x_2, z_1) = \max\{0.54, 0.24, 0.12\} = 0.54$

**Step 5: Compute $T(x_2, z_2)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1: \quad & 0.9 \times 0.1 = 0.09 \\
y_2: \quad & 0.3 \times 0.5 = 0.15 \\
y_3: \quad & 0.4 \times 0.9 = 0.36
\end{aligned}
$$

Maximum: $T(x_2, z_2) = \max\{0.09, 0.15, 0.36\} = 0.36$

**Step 6: Assemble the result matrix.**

$$T = R \circ_p S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.56 & 0.45 \\ 0.54 & 0.36 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification:** Each entry is in $[0,1]$. ✓ Dimensions are compatible. ✓

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Extension Principle (Exhaustive Derivation)

**Problem:** Let $A$ be a fuzzy set on $\mathbb{R}$ defined by:
- $\mu_A(1) = 0.4$, $\mu_A(2) = 0.7$, $\mu_A(3) = 1.0$, $\mu_A(4) = 0.6$, $\mu_A(5) = 0.3$, and $\mu_A(x) = 0$ elsewhere.

Apply the **Extension Principle** to compute $B = f(A)$ where $f(x) = x^2$.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Determine the domain of $B$.**
$f$ maps $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$ to $\{1, 4, 9, 16, 25\}$. So the support of $B$ is $\{1, 4, 9, 16, 25\}$.

**Step 2: Compute $\mu_B$ at each output value using the Extension Principle.**

For $y = 1$: $f^{-1}(1) = \{1\}$ (only $x=1$ satisfies $x^2 = 1$).
$$\mu_B(1) = \mu_A(1) = 0.4$$

For $y = 4$: $f^{-1}(4) = \{2\}$ (only $x=2$ satisfies $x^2 = 4$).
$$\mu_B(4) = \mu_A(2) = 0.7$$

For $y = 9$: $f^{-1}(9) = \{3\}$.
$$\mu_B(9) = \mu_A(3) = 1.0$$

For $y = 16$: $f^{-1}(16) = \{4\}$.
$$\mu_B(16) = \mu_A(4) = 0.6$$

For $y = 25$: $f^{-1}(25) = \{5\}$.
$$\mu_B(25) = \mu_A(5) = 0.3$$

**Step 3: State the result.**

$$B = \{0.4/1, 0.7/4, 1.0/9, 0.6/16, 0.3/25\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Since $f(x) = x^2$ is **one-to-one** on positive reals, the Extension Principle here reduces to direct substitution. For **non-injective** functions (e.g., $f(x) = x^2 - x$), the supremum over $f^{-1}(y)$ is essential.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Addition of Triangular Fuzzy Numbers (Using α-Cuts)

**Problem:** Given $A = (2, 5, 8)$ and $B = (1, 4, 7)$ as Triangular Fuzzy Numbers, compute $A + B$ using α-cuts.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Write the α-cuts of $A$ and $B$ in interval form.**

For a TFN $(a, b, c)$, the α-cut is:

$$[A]_\alpha = \left[ a + (b-a)\alpha, \; c - (c-b)\alpha \right] = [a + \alpha(b-a), \; c - \alpha(c-b)]$$

**Step 2: Compute $[A]_\alpha$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[A]_\alpha &= [2 + \alpha(5-2), \; 8 - \alpha(8-5)] \\
&= [2 + 3\alpha, \; 8 - 3\alpha]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $[B]_\alpha$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[B]_\alpha &= [1 + \alpha(4-1), \; 7 - \alpha(7-4)] \\
&= [1 + 3\alpha, \; 7 - 3\alpha]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply interval addition at each α level.**

$$[A+B]_\alpha = [A]_\alpha + [B]_\alpha = \left[ (2+3\alpha) + (1+3\alpha), \; (8-3\alpha) + (7-3\alpha) \right]$$

Simplify:

$$[A+B]_\alpha = [3 + 6\alpha, \; 15 - 6\alpha]$$

**Step 5: Read off the parameters of the resulting TFN.**

Comparing with the general form $[a' + (b'-a')\alpha, \; c' - (c'-b')\alpha]$:
- $a' = 3$
- $b' - a' = 6 \Rightarrow b' = 9$
- $c' - b' = 6 \Rightarrow c' = 15$

**Step 6: Final answer.**

$$A + B = (3, 9, 15)$$

**Verification (using the standard rule for TFN addition):**

$$(a_1, b_1, c_1) \oplus (a_2, b_2, c_2) = (a_1+a_2, \; b_1+b_2, \; c_1+c_2)$$

$$(2,5,8) \oplus (1,4,7) = (3, 9, 15) \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Always show the **two independent methods** (α-cut method AND direct parametric method) to earn full marks. Examiners give partial credit if only one method is shown.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Composition Methods

| Property | Max-Min | Max-Product |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| T-norm used | $\min$ | $\cdot$ (product) |
| Penalty for weak links | Less severe (uses min) | Severe (uses product) |
| Idempotence | Yes | No (in general) |
| Distributivity over ∪ | Yes | Yes |
| Common in | Fuzzy logic inference | Fuzzy cognitive maps, weighting |
| KTU exam frequency | High | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Workflow of Fuzzy Arithmetic via α-Cuts

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete pipeline for performing arithmetic on fuzzy numbers using the α-cut method — the standard KTU 2024 board approach.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Fuzzy Numbers A and B] --> B[Define Membership Functions muA and muB]
    B --> C[Choose Alpha Levels: alpha in 0,1]
    C --> D[Compute Alpha-cut Intervals]
    D --> D1["Compute A_alpha: lowerA alpha, upperA alpha"]
    D --> D2["Compute B_alpha: lowerB alpha, upperB alpha"]
    D1 --> E[Apply Interval Arithmetic]
    D2 --> E
    E --> E1["Add: lowerA lowerB, upperA upperB"]
    E --> E2["Subtract: lowerA upperB, upperA lowerB"]
    E --> E3["Multiply: min of corner products, max of corner products"]
    E --> E4["Divide: apply interval division rules"]
    E1 --> F[Assemble Result Interval at Each Alpha]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Reconstruct Fuzzy Number C]
    G --> H[Plot Membership Function of C]
    H --> I[Final Output: Fuzzy Number C]
    I --> J[Verify: Check Convexity, Normality, Support]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each block represents a transformation stage. The diamond-shaped decision blocks (D1/D2, E1–E4) represent parallel branches; the system selects the appropriate arithmetic operation per problem requirements. The terminal block "Verify" represents the post-processing validation step.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Extension Principle Workflow

The diagram below maps the operational flow of the **Extension Principle** when applied to a generic function $f$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Fuzzy Set A on Domain X] --> B[Identify Function f: X to Y]
    B --> C[For each output y in Y]
    C --> D[Compute Pre-image f-inverse of y]
    D --> E{Is Pre-image Empty?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Set muB of y equal to 0]
    E -->|No| G[Collect muA of x for all x in Pre-image]
    G --> H[Take Supremum: max muA of x]
    H --> I[Assign muB of y equal to sup value]
    F --> J[Move to Next y in Y]
    I --> J
    J --> K{All y Processed?}
    K -->|No| C
    K -->|Yes| L[Output Fuzzy Set B on Y]
    L --> M[Verify: B is Fuzzy Number if A is Fuzzy Number]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Max-Product Composition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Fuzzy Relation R on X cross Y] --> B[Input Fuzzy Relation S on Y cross Z]
    B --> C[Initialize Result Matrix T of size m x p]
    C --> D[For each x in X]
    D --> E[For each z in Z]
    E --> F[For each y in Y: compute R x y times S y z]
    F --> G[Take Max over y: max of products]
    G --> H[Store T x z equal to max]
    H --> I{All x,z Processed?}
    I -->|No| E
    I -->|Yes| J[Output Fuzzy Relation T on X cross Z]
    J --> K[Compare with Max-Min for Verification]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Insight:** The Max-Product pipeline is **algorithmic** and scales as $O(|X| \cdot |Y| \cdot |Z|)$. For continuous domains, the algorithm must be discretized or solved analytically using parametric representations of fuzzy numbers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Extension Principle as introduced by Zadeh. State its significance in fuzzy set theory.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Extension Principle**, introduced by **Lotfi A. Zadeh** in 1975, is a fundamental method that generalizes crisp mathematical functions to operate on fuzzy sets. Given a function $f : X \to Y$ and a fuzzy set $A$ in $X$ with membership function $\mu_A(x)$, the image $B = f(A)$ in $Y$ is defined as:
$$\mu_B(y) = \sup_{x \in f^{-1}(y)} \mu_A(x) \quad \text{if } f^{-1}(y) \neq \emptyset, \text{ else } 0$$
**Significance:** It enables crisp mathematical operations (arithmetic, algebra, calculus) to be performed on imprecise/vague inputs, forming the foundation of fuzzy arithmetic, fuzzy control, and decision-making under uncertainty.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Max-Product and Max-Min fuzzy composition with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Max-Min | Max-Product |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| T-norm used | $\min$ operator | Algebraic product ($\cdot$) |
| Formula | $\max_y \min[\mu_R, \mu_S]$ | $\max_y [\mu_R \cdot \mu_S]$ |
| Behavior | Weakest link dominates | Combined strength used |
| Idempotent | Yes | No |

**Example:** For $R(0.5, 0.8)$ and $S(0.6, 0.3)$ — Max-Min: $\max(\min(0.5,0.6), \min(0.8,0.3)) = \max(0.5, 0.3) = 0.5$. Max-Product: $\max(0.5 \cdot 0.6, 0.8 \cdot 0.3) = \max(0.30, 0.24) = 0.30$.

---

### **PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define a **Fuzzy Number**. Explain the properties of normality, convexity, and bounded support. Derive the α-cut representation of a **Triangular Fuzzy Number** $A = (a, b, c)$ and a **Trapezoidal Fuzzy Number** $A = (a, b, c, d)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given two fuzzy numbers $A = (1, 3, 5)$ and $B = (2, 4, 6)$, compute $A + B$, $A - B$, and $2A$ using the **α-cut method**. Show all steps clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part Solution:**

**Definition of Fuzzy Number:** A fuzzy number $A$ on $\mathbb{R}$ is a fuzzy set satisfying:
- **Normality:** $\sup_x \mu_A(x) = 1$ (a most representative value exists)
- **Convexity:** $\mu_A(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda)x_2) \geq \min[\mu_A(x_1), \mu_A(x_2)]$
- **Bounded support:** $\{x : \mu_A(x) > 0\}$ is bounded
- **Upper semi-continuous** membership function

**Properties Engineering Meaning:**
- Normality: the value with full membership is the "most likely" estimate.
- Convexity: ensures α-cuts are intervals — necessary for arithmetic.
- Bounded support: guarantees finiteness, making fuzzy numbers computationally tractable.

**α-cut of TFN $A = (a, b, c)$:**

The membership function is:
$$\mu_A(x) = \begin{cases} (x-a)/(b-a) & a \leq x \leq b \\ (c-x)/(c-b) & b \leq x \leq c \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

For $\mu_A(x) \geq \alpha$:
- Left side: $(x-a)/(b-a) \geq \alpha \Rightarrow x \geq a + \alpha(b-a)$
- Right side: $(c-x)/(c-b) \geq \alpha \Rightarrow x \leq c - \alpha(c-b)$

Therefore:
$$[A]_\alpha = [a + \alpha(b-a), \; c - \alpha(c-b)]$$

**α-cut of TrFN $A = (a, b, c, d)$:**

$$\mu_A(x) = \begin{cases} (x-a)/(b-a) & a \leq x \leq b \\ 1 & b \leq x \leq c \\ (d-x)/(d-c) & c \leq x \leq d \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Therefore:
$$[A]_\alpha = [a + \alpha(b-a), \; d - \alpha(d-c)]$$

**(b) Part Solution:**

**Step 1: α-cuts of $A = (1, 3, 5)$ and $B = (2, 4, 6)$.**

$$[A]_\alpha = [1 + 2\alpha, \; 5 - 2\alpha]$$
$$[B]_\alpha = [2 + 2\alpha, \; 6 - 2\alpha]$$

**Step 2: Compute $A + B$ (Interval Addition).**

$$[A+B]_\alpha = [(1+2\alpha) + (2+2\alpha), \; (5-2\alpha) + (6-2\alpha)] = [3 + 4\alpha, \; 11 - 4\alpha]$$

**Result:** $A + B = (3, 7, 11)$

**Step 3: Compute $A - B$ (Interval Subtraction).**

For intervals, $[A]_\alpha - [B]_\alpha = [\underline{a} - \overline{b}, \; \overline{a} - \underline{b}]$:

$$[A-B]_\alpha = [(1+2\alpha) - (6-2\alpha), \; (5-2\alpha) - (2+2\alpha)] = [-5 + 4\alpha, \; 3 - 4\alpha]$$

**Result:** $A - B = (-5, -1, 3)$

**Step 4: Compute $2A$ (Scalar Multiplication).**

$$[2A]_\alpha = [2(1+2\alpha), \; 2(5-2\alpha)] = [2 + 4\alpha, \; 10 - 4\alpha]$$

**Result:** $2A = (2, 6, 10)$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** 
> 1. Students **frequently confuse interval addition with subtraction** — for $[A]_\alpha - [B]_\alpha$, the lower bound uses $\overline{B}$ and the upper bound uses $\underline{B}$. Drawing the wrong sign loses 3–4 marks.
> 2. Always show the **α-cut form** explicitly. Writing only the final answer $(3, 7, 11)$ without the intermediate step loses 2 marks for "missing working."
> 3. For scalar multiplication, the rule is $\mu_{kA}(x) = \mu_A(x/k)$, not $\mu_A(kx)$. Do not reverse the order.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** State and explain **Zadeh's Extension Principle** for fuzzy sets. Apply the principle to compute $B = f(A)$ where $A = \{0.2/1, 0.5/2, 0.8/3, 1.0/4, 0.6/5\}$ and $f(x) = 2x + 1$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **Max-Product composition** $T = R \circ_p S$ for the given fuzzy relations:
$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.9 \\ 0.7 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.8 & 0.1 \\ 0.6 & 0.3 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix}$$
Compare your result with the **Max-Min composition** of the same relations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part Solution:**

**Statement of Extension Principle:** (Recall the formal definition from Section 1.2.)

**Application:**

**Step 1: Apply $f$ to each element.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(1) &= 2(1) + 1 = 3 \\
f(2) &= 2(2) + 1 = 5 \\
f(3) &= 2(3) + 1 = 7 \\
f(4) &= 2(4) + 1 = 9 \\
f(5) &= 2(5) + 1 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Since $f$ is one-to-one, the pre-image of each output is a singleton. Apply the Extension Principle directly.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_B(3) &= \mu_A(1) = 0.2 \\
\mu_B(5) &= \mu_A(2) = 0.5 \\
\mu_B(7) &= \mu_A(3) = 0.8 \\
\mu_B(9) &= \mu_A(4) = 1.0 \\
\mu_B(11) &= \mu_A(5) = 0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**

$$B = \{0.2/3, 0.5/5, 0.8/7, 1.0/9, 0.6/11\}$$

**(b) Part Solution:**

**Step 1: Verify dimensions.** $R$ is $2 \times 2$, $S$ is $2 \times 3$. The shared index has size 2. Result $T$ is $2 \times 3$.

**Step 2: Max-Product Composition $T = R \circ_p S$.**

Compute $T(x_i, z_j) = \max_y [R(x_i, y) \cdot S(y, z_j)]$.

For $T(x_1, z_1)$:
- $y_1: 0.4 \times 0.5 = 0.20$
- $y_2: 0.9 \times 0.6 = 0.54$
- $T(x_1, z_1) = 0.54$

For $T(x_1, z_2)$:
- $y_1: 0.4 \times 0.8 = 0.32$
- $y_2: 0.9 \times 0.3 = 0.27$
- $T(x_1, z_2) = 0.32$

For $T(x_1, z_3)$:
- $y_1: 0.4 \times 0.1 = 0.04$
- $y_2: 0.9 \times 0.9 = 0.81$
- $T(x_1, z_3) = 0.81$

For $T(x_2, z_1)$:
- $y_1: 0.7 \times 0.5 = 0.35$
- $y_2: 0.2 \times 0.6 = 0.12$
- $T(x_2, z_1) = 0.35$

For $T(x_2, z_2)$:
- $y_1: 0.7 \times 0.8 = 0.56$
- $y_2: 0.2 \times 0.3 = 0.06$
- $T(x_2, z_2) = 0.56$

For $T(x_2, z_3)$:
- $y_1: 0.7 \times 0.1 = 0.07$
- $y_2: 0.2 \times 0.9 = 0.18$
- $T(x_2, z_3) = 0.18$

**Result — Max-Product:**
$$T_{\text{prod}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.54 & 0.32 & 0.81 \\ 0.35 & 0.56 & 0.18 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Max-Min Composition $T' = R \circ S$ (for comparison).**

For $T'(x_1, z_1)$: $\max(\min(0.4, 0.5), \min(0.9, 0.6)) = \max(0.4, 0.6) = 0.6$

For $T'(x_1, z_2)$: $\max(\min(0.4, 0.8), \min(0.9, 0.3)) = \max(0.4, 0.3) = 0.4$

For $T'(x_1, z_3)$: $\max(\min(0.4, 0.1), \min(0.9, 0.9)) = \max(0.1, 0.9) = 0.9$

For $T'(x_2, z_1)$: $\max(\min(0.7, 0.5), \min(0.2, 0.6)) = \max(0.5, 0.2) = 0.5$

For $T'(x_2, z_2)$: $\max(\min(0.7, 0.8), \min(0.2, 0.3)) = \max(0.7, 0.2) = 0.7$

For $T'(x_2, z_3)$: $\max(\min(0.7, 0.1), \min(0.2, 0.9)) = \max(0.1, 0.2) = 0.2$

**Result — Max-Min:**
$$T_{\min} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 & 0.9 \\ 0.5 & 0.7 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Comparison:** The Max-Min values are consistently **higher or equal** to Max-Product values for these specific matrices, illustrating the general principle that Min is "more forgiving" (it only takes the weakest link) while Product "punishes" weak links more severely.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** 
> 1. In Max-Product, the operation between $\mu_R$ and $\mu_S$ is **multiplication**, not minimum. Writing $\min(0.4, 0.5)$ instead of $0.4 \times 0.5$ means the student has confused the two composition methods — **deduct 4 marks**.
> 2. In Max-Min, students often mistakenly use the **product** as the inner operator. This is a common error.
> 3. Always **state the result explicitly** in matrix form. Writing only the entries as a list (without a matrix) loses 1 mark for presentation.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- **Max-Product Composition:** $T(x,z) = \max_y [\mu_R(x,y) \cdot \mu_S(y,z)]$. Multiplicative inner operator.
- **Max-Min Composition:** $T(x,z) = \max_y \min[\mu_R(x,y), \mu_S(y,z)]$. Minimum inner operator.
- **Extension Principle:** $\mu_B(y) = \sup_{x \in f^{-1}(y)} \mu_A(x)$; zero if pre-image is empty.
- **Fuzzy Number:** Normal + Convex + Bounded support + Upper semi-continuous.
- **TFN $(a, b, c)$:** α-cut is $[a + \alpha(b-a), \; c - \alpha(c-b)]$.
- **TrFN $(a, b, c, d)$:** α-cut is $[a + \alpha(b-a), \; d - \alpha(d-c)]$.
- **Gaussian FN:** $\mu(x) = \exp(-(x-m)^2 / 2\sigma^2)$.
- **Fuzzy Arithmetic (Addition):** $(a_1, b_1, c_1) \oplus (a_2, b_2, c_2) = (a_1+a_2, b_1+b_2, c_1+c_2)$ for TFNs.
- **Fuzzy Arithmetic (Subtraction):** $(a_1, b_1, c_1) \ominus (a_2, b_2, c_2) = (a_1-c_2, b_1-b_2, c_1-a_2)$ for TFNs.
- **Scalar Multiplication:** $k \cdot (a, b, c) = (ka, kb, kc)$ for $k > 0$.
- **Key Theorem:** $[A \circ B]_\alpha = [A]_\alpha \circ [B]_\alpha$ for arithmetic operations.
- **Common Pitfall #1:** Confusing Max-Product with Max-Min (using wrong inner operator).
- **Common Pitfall #2:** Wrong sign in interval subtraction (lower bound uses $\overline{B}$, not $\underline{B}$).
- **Common Pitfall #3:** Writing parametric TFN result without showing α-cut derivation.
- **Real-World Applications:** Fuzzy controllers, sensor fusion, decision analysis, risk assessment, expert systems.
- **Key Properties:** Max-Product is **associative** but **not commutative** or **idempotent** in general.
- **Sub & Sup Rule:** When multiple inputs map to the same output (non-injective $f$), the **supremum** (greatest lower bound of upper bounds) is used; the **infimum** (least upper bound) appears in inverse problems.
- **Convexity Theorem:** A fuzzy set is a fuzzy number **if and only if** every α-cut is a non-empty compact interval — this is the most useful test.
- **Closure Property:** The set of all fuzzy numbers is **closed** under addition and scalar multiplication (for $k > 0$), but **not under division** in general.
- **Examination Tip (KTU 2024):** When asked to "compute" any fuzzy operation, **always show both the α-cut method AND the parametric shortcut** if applicable — this demonstrates deep understanding and earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arithmetic Operations on Fuzzy Numbers, Fuzzy Reasoning - Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP) and Generalized Modus Tollens (GMT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST753 - GAME THEORY AND MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arithmetic Operations on Fuzzy Numbers, Fuzzy Reasoning - Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP) and Generalized Modus Tollens (GMT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fuzzy Numbers — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuzzy Number:** A fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ defined on the real line $\mathbb{R}$ is called a **Fuzzy Number** if its membership function $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) : \mathbb{R} \to [0,1]$ satisfies the following three axioms:
> 1. **Normality:** $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1$ for at least one $x \in \mathbb{R}$ (the peak of the fuzzy number).
> 2. **Convexity:** All $\alpha$-cuts of $\tilde{A}$ are closed (and bounded) intervals of $\mathbb{R}$, i.e., $A_\alpha = [a_1(\alpha), a_2(\alpha)]$ for every $\alpha \in (0,1]$.
> 3. **Compact Support:** The support of $\tilde{A}$ (the set where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0$) must be bounded.

The most common canonical example is the **triangular fuzzy number** $\tilde{A} = (l, m, u)$, whose membership function is piecewise linear rising from $0$ at $x=l$ to $1$ at $x=m$, and falling back to $0$ at $x=u$.

### 1.2 α-Cut (Alpha-Cut) — The Workhorse of Fuzzy Arithmetic

> [!NOTE]
> **α-Cut Definition:** The $\alpha$-cut of a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ is the crisp (non-fuzzy) set
> $$A_\alpha = \{x \in \mathbb{R} : \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha\}, \quad \alpha \in (0,1].$$
> The **strong α-cut** uses strict inequality: $A_{\alpha}^{+} = \{x : \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > \alpha\}$.

For a fuzzy number, every $\alpha$-cut is a **closed interval** $A_\alpha = [a_1(\alpha), a_2(\alpha)]$. Reducing fuzzy arithmetic to **interval arithmetic** on these α-cuts is the central engineering trick taught at KTU.

### 1.3 Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GMP (Fuzzy Extension of Classical Modus Ponens):**
> - **Premise 1 (Rule):** If $X$ is $\tilde{A}$ then $Y$ is $\tilde{B}$.
> - **Premise 2 (Fact):** $X$ is $\tilde{A}'$ (a fuzzy set close to $\tilde{A}$).
> - **Conclusion:** $Y$ is $\tilde{B}'$.
>
> Formally, the conclusion is computed via the **Compositional Rule of Inference (CRI):**
> $$\tilde{B}' = \tilde{A}' \circ (\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B})$$
> where $\circ$ is the **sup-min composition** and $\times$ denotes the Cartesian product fuzzy relation.

### 1.4 Generalized Modus Tollens (GMT) — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **GMT (Fuzzy Extension of Classical Modus Tollens):**
> - **Premise 1 (Rule):** If $X$ is $\tilde{A}$ then $Y$ is $\tilde{B}$.
> - **Premise 2 (Fact):** $Y$ is $\tilde{B}'$ (which is *not equal* to $\tilde{B}$).
> - **Conclusion:** $X$ is $\tilde{A}'$.
>
> The conclusion is again obtained using CRI: $\tilde{A}' = \tilde{B}' \circ (\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B})^T$, where the transpose swaps antecedent and consequent.

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **weather forecaster using imprecise data**:

| Classical Logic | Fuzzy Logic Analogy |
|---|---|
| "If temperature is exactly 100°C, then water boils" | "If temperature is **approximately 100°C** (say around 98–102°C), then water **roughly boils**" |
| Premise fact: "Temperature IS exactly 100°C" | Premise fact: "Temperature is **near 100°C** but not exact" |
| Conclusion: "Water boils" | Conclusion (GMP): "Water **approximately boils**" |

The GMP lets us draw a *plausible* conclusion from a *plausible* fact — exactly how humans reason with words. The **arithmetic operations** allow us to *combine* such imprecise numbers (e.g., "around 5 + around 7 ≈ around 12") rigorously.

### 1.6 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Universe of Discourse ($U$):** The crisp underlying set (often $\mathbb{R}$).
- **Core:** Set of points where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1$.
- **Support:** Set of points where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0$.
- **Height** of a fuzzy set: $h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_x \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$. For a fuzzy number, $h = \mathbf{1}$ by the normality condition.
- **Modal Value:** The point in the core (used as the "representative" crisp value in KTU numerical problems).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The α-Cut Decomposition Theorem — Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decomposition Theorem:** Every fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ can be reconstructed from its $\alpha$-cuts using the **Resolution Identity**:
> $$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \sup_{\alpha \in (0,1]} \bigl[\alpha \cdot \mathbb{I}_{A_\alpha}(x)\bigr]$$
> where $\mathbb{I}_{A_\alpha}(x) = 1$ if $x \in A_\alpha$ and $0$ otherwise.

This theorem is what allows us to *reduce* fuzzy arithmetic to interval arithmetic — a fundamental exam favorite.

### 2.2 Arithmetic Operations via α-Cuts

Let $\tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{B}$ be two fuzzy numbers with α-cuts $A_\alpha = [a_1(\alpha), a_2(\alpha)]$ and $B_\alpha = [b_1(\alpha), b_2(\alpha)]$. Define the binary operation $*$ on the fuzzy numbers. Then the α-cut of the result is computed using **interval arithmetic**:

#### (a) Addition: $\tilde{C} = \tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}$
$$C_\alpha = [a_1(\alpha) + b_1(\alpha), \quad a_2(\alpha) + b_2(\alpha)]$$
The left endpoints add; the right endpoints add. **No sign considerations needed.**

#### (b) Subtraction: $\tilde{C} = \tilde{A} \ominus \tilde{B}$
$$C_\alpha = [a_1(\alpha) - b_2(\alpha), \quad a_2(\alpha) - b_1(\alpha)]$$
**Critical:** left endpoint uses the **right** endpoint of the subtrahend, and vice versa.

#### (c) Multiplication: $\tilde{C} = \tilde{A} \otimes \tilde{B}$
The bounds are formed from the **four products** of interval endpoints; then the **minimum** is the lower bound and the **maximum** is the upper bound.
$$C_\alpha = \bigl[\min\{a_1 b_1, a_1 b_2, a_2 b_1, a_2 b_2\}, \quad \max\{a_1 b_1, a_1 b_2, a_2 b_1, a_2 b_2\}\bigr]$$
For **non-negative fuzzy numbers** (the most common exam case where $a_1, a_2, b_1, b_2 \geq 0$), this simplifies dramatically:
$$C_\alpha = [a_1(\alpha)\cdot b_1(\alpha), \quad a_2(\alpha)\cdot b_2(\alpha)]$$

#### (d) Division: $\tilde{C} = \tilde{A} \oslash \tilde{B}$
For $0 \notin B_\alpha$ (i.e., $\tilde{B}$ does not contain zero in its support):
$$C_\alpha = \Bigl[\min\Bigl\{\tfrac{a_1}{b_1},\tfrac{a_1}{b_2},\tfrac{a_2}{b_1},\tfrac{a_2}{b_2}\Bigr\}, \quad \max\Bigl\{\tfrac{a_1}{b_1},\tfrac{a_1}{b_2},\tfrac{a_2}{b_1},\tfrac{a_2}{b_2}\Bigr\}\Bigr]$$
For **positive fuzzy numbers**:
$$C_\alpha = \Bigl[\tfrac{a_1(\alpha)}{b_2(\alpha)}, \quad \tfrac{a_2(\alpha)}{b_1(\alpha)}\Bigr]$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Quick Reference

| Operation | Formula for $C_\alpha$ (General Case) | Simplified (when $\tilde{A}, \tilde{B} > 0$) |
|---|---|---|
| Addition $\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}$ | $[a_1+b_1, \; a_2+b_2]$ | $[a_1+b_1, \; a_2+b_2]$ |
| Subtraction $\tilde{A} \ominus \tilde{B}$ | $[a_1-b_2, \; a_2-b_1]$ | $[a_1-b_2, \; a_2-b_1]$ |
| Multiplication $\tilde{A} \otimes \tilde{B}$ | $[\min, \max]$ over all four products | $[a_1 b_1, \; a_2 b_2]$ |
| Division $\tilde{A} \oslash \tilde{B}$ | $[\min, \max]$ over all four quotients | $[a_1 / b_2, \; a_2 / b_1]$ |
| Scalar multiple $k\tilde{A}$ ($k>0$) | $[k a_1, \; k a_2]$ | $[k a_1, \; k a_2]$ |
| Scalar multiple $k\tilde{A}$ ($k<0$) | $[k a_2, \; k a_1]$ | $[k a_2, \; k a_1]$ |
| GMP Conclusion | $\tilde{B}' = \tilde{A}' \circ \tilde{R}$, with $\tilde{R} = \tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}$ | $\mu_{\tilde{B}'}(y) = \sup_x \min\{\mu_{\tilde{A}'}(x), \mu_{\tilde{R}}(x,y)\}$ |
| GMT Conclusion | $\tilde{A}' = \tilde{B}' \circ \tilde{R}^T$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A}'}(x) = \sup_y \min\{\mu_{\tilde{B}'}(y), \mu_{\tilde{R}}(x,y)\}$ |

### 2.4 The Extension Principle (Alternative Foundation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Zadeh's Extension Principle:** Given a function $f: X_1 \times X_2 \times \dots \times X_n \to Y$ and fuzzy sets $\tilde{A}_i$ on $X_i$, the image fuzzy set $\tilde{B} = f(\tilde{A}_1, \dots, \tilde{A}_n)$ has membership:
> $$\mu_{\tilde{B}}(y) = \sup_{(x_1,\dots,x_n): y = f(x_1,\dots,x_n)} \min_i \mu_{\tilde{A}_i}(x_i)$$
> This is mathematically equivalent to the α-cut interval arithmetic approach for fuzzy numbers.

### 2.5 Why and How — Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Economics & Finance:** Portfolio optimization with imprecise returns — "low risk" and "moderate return" become fuzzy numbers you can add and divide.
- **Control Systems:** Fuzzy PID controllers depend entirely on GMP to map a fuzzy error input to a fuzzy control output.
- **Decision Theory / Game Theory (your course context):** Player utilities and payoff matrices are often **linguistic** ("high profit," "moderate loss"); GMP enables reasoning under such imprecision in mechanism design.
- **AI / Expert Systems:** Medical diagnosis engines use GMT in reverse reasoning ("The disease is NOT dengue, and dengue causes symptom X, therefore symptom X must be caused by something else").
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Arithmetic on Triangular Fuzzy Numbers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (typical 7-mark KTU style):** Given two triangular fuzzy numbers $\tilde{A} = (2, 4, 6)$ and $\tilde{B} = (1, 3, 5)$. Compute:
> (a) $\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}$ — Sum
> (b) $\tilde{A} \ominus \tilde{B}$ — Difference
> (c) $\tilde{A} \otimes \tilde{B}$ — Product (positive case)

#### Step 1 — Derive the α-Cut Expressions

For any triangular fuzzy number $\tilde{T} = (l, m, u)$, the membership function is:

$$\mu_{\tilde{T}}(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{x - l}{m - l}, & l \leq x \leq m \\[6pt] \dfrac{u - x}{u - m}, & m \leq x \leq u \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Setting $\mu_{\tilde{T}}(x) = \alpha$, we solve for the interval endpoints.

**For $\tilde{A} = (2, 4, 6)$:**

On the left limb $l \leq x \leq m$ (i.e., $2 \leq x \leq 4$):

$$\alpha = \frac{x - 2}{4 - 2} = \frac{x - 2}{2} \implies x = 2 + 2\alpha$$

So $a_1(\alpha) = 2 + 2\alpha$.

On the right limb $m \leq x \leq u$ (i.e., $4 \leq x \leq 6$):

$$\alpha = \frac{6 - x}{6 - 4} = \frac{6 - x}{2} \implies x = 6 - 2\alpha$$

So $a_2(\alpha) = 6 - 2\alpha$.

Therefore:
$$A_\alpha = [2 + 2\alpha, \; 6 - 2\alpha]$$

**For $\tilde{B} = (1, 3, 5)$:**

By identical algebra:

$$b_1(\alpha) = 1 + 2\alpha, \quad b_2(\alpha) = 5 - 2\alpha$$
$$B_\alpha = [1 + 2\alpha, \; 5 - 2\alpha]$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Sum $\tilde{C} = \tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}$

$$C_\alpha = [a_1(\alpha) + b_1(\alpha), \; a_2(\alpha) + b_2(\alpha)]$$

Substituting:

$$C_\alpha = [(2 + 2\alpha) + (1 + 2\alpha), \; (6 - 2\alpha) + (5 - 2\alpha)]$$

$$C_\alpha = [3 + 4\alpha, \; 11 - 4\alpha]$$

To recover the triangular form, we find the **membership values** at the endpoints:

- When $x = 3$: $\alpha$ satisfies $3 = 3 + 4\alpha \Rightarrow \alpha = 0$. So $\mu_{\tilde{C}}(3) = 0$.
- When $x = 11$: $\alpha$ satisfies $11 = 11 - 4\alpha \Rightarrow \alpha = 0$. So $\mu_{\tilde{C}}(11) = 0$.
- The peak occurs where $a_1 + b_1 = a_2 + b_2$, i.e., $3 + 4\alpha = 11 - 4\alpha \Rightarrow \alpha = 1$, giving $x = 7$.

$$\boxed{\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B} = (3, 7, 11)}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Difference $\tilde{D} = \tilde{A} \ominus \tilde{B}$

$$D_\alpha = [a_1(\alpha) - b_2(\alpha), \; a_2(\alpha) - b_1(\alpha)]$$

$$D_\alpha = [(2 + 2\alpha) - (5 - 2\alpha), \; (6 - 2\alpha) - (1 + 2\alpha)]$$

$$D_\alpha = [-3 + 4\alpha, \; 5 - 4\alpha]$$

Endpoints at $\alpha = 0$: $D_0 = [-3, 5]$. Peak at $\alpha = 1$: $x = 1$.

$$\boxed{\tilde{A} \ominus \tilde{B} = (-3, 1, 5)}$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the Product $\tilde{E} = \tilde{A} \otimes \tilde{B}$ (Positive Case)

Since $\tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{B}$ have **non-negative supports** ($a_1, a_2, b_1, b_2 \geq 0$ for all $\alpha \in [0,1]$), we use the simplified rule:

$$E_\alpha = [a_1(\alpha) \cdot b_1(\alpha), \; a_2(\alpha) \cdot b_2(\alpha)]$$

Lower bound:

$$a_1(\alpha) \cdot b_1(\alpha) = (2 + 2\alpha)(1 + 2\alpha) = 2 + 4\alpha + 2\alpha + 4\alpha^2 = 2 + 6\alpha + 4\alpha^2$$

Upper bound:

$$a_2(\alpha) \cdot b_2(\alpha) = (6 - 2\alpha)(5 - 2\alpha) = 30 - 12\alpha - 10\alpha + 4\alpha^2 = 30 - 22\alpha + 4\alpha^2$$

At $\alpha = 0$: bounds are $2$ and $30$.
At $\alpha = 1$: bounds are $12$ and $12$, so peak is $12$.

The membership function is therefore **non-linear** (quadratic in $\alpha$), so the product is a **non-triangular fuzzy number**:

$$\boxed{\tilde{E} = \tilde{A} \otimes \tilde{B}, \quad \mu_{\tilde{E}}(x) \text{ defined implicitly via } E_\alpha = [2 + 6\alpha + 4\alpha^2, \; 30 - 22\alpha + 4\alpha^2]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** The product of two triangular fuzzy numbers is generally **NOT triangular**. It is a bell-shaped fuzzy number with quadratic α-cuts. Examiners frequently test this with "show that the product is not triangular."

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP) via CRI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** Consider the fuzzy rule:
> *"If $X$ is small, then $Y$ is large."*
> Suppose "small" is modeled as $\tilde{A}$ with discrete universe $X = \{1, 2, 3\}$, and "large" is modeled as $\tilde{B}$ with $Y = \{1, 2, 3\}$. Specifically:
> $$\tilde{A} = \{1/1, \; 0.6/2, \; 0.2/3\}, \qquad \tilde{B} = \{0.2/1, \; 0.6/2, \; 1/3\}$$
> Given the new fact $\tilde{A}' = \{0.5/1, \; 1/2, \; 0.5/3\}$, find $\tilde{B}'$ using GMP with sup-min composition.

#### Step 1 — Compute the Fuzzy Relation $\tilde{R} = \tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}$

The Cartesian product gives a relation $R(x,y) = \min\{\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(y)\}$, producing a $3 \times 3$ matrix:

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} \min(1, 0.2) & \min(1, 0.6) & \min(1, 1) \\ \min(0.6, 0.2) & \min(0.6, 0.6) & \min(0.6, 1) \\ \min(0.2, 0.2) & \min(0.2, 0.6) & \min(0.2, 1) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.6 & 1 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 & 0.6 \\ 0.2 & 0.2 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 2 — Apply Sup-Min Composition: $\tilde{B}' = \tilde{A}' \circ R$

The sup-min composition is computed as:

$$\mu_{\tilde{B}'}(y_j) = \sup_{x_i} \min\{\mu_{\tilde{A}'}(x_i), R(x_i, y_j)\}$$

For $y_1 = 1$ (column 1 of $R$):

$$\mu_{\tilde{B}'}(1) = \max\{\min(0.5, 0.2), \; \min(1, 0.2), \; \min(0.5, 0.2)\}$$
$$= \max\{0.2, \; 0.2, \; 0.2\} = 0.2$$

For $y_2 = 2$ (column 2 of $R$):

$$\mu_{\tilde{B}'}(2) = \max\{\min(0.5, 0.6), \; \min(1, 0.6), \; \min(0.5, 0.2)\}$$
$$= \max\{0.5, \; 0.6, \; 0.2\} = 0.6$$

For $y_3 = 3$ (column 3 of $R$):

$$\mu_{\tilde{B}'}(3) = \max\{\min(0.5, 1), \; \min(1, 0.6), \; \min(0.5, 0.2)\}$$
$$= \max\{0.5, \; 0.6, \; 0.2\} = 0.6$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\tilde{B}' = \{0.2/1, \; 0.6/2, \; 0.6/3\}}$$

#### Step 3 — Interpretation

The conclusion $\tilde{B}'$ indicates that $Y$ is "moderately to highly large." Notice $\tilde{B}'$ is **close to but not equal to** the original $\tilde{B} = \{0.2, 0.6, 1\}$, reflecting that $\tilde{A}'$ is "somewhat similar" to $\tilde{A}$ — neither identical nor completely different. This is the hallmark of fuzzy (approximate) reasoning.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Generalized Modus Tollens (GMT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** Using the same rule and fuzzy sets as in Example 2, given that the observed output is $\tilde{B}' = \{0.8/1, \; 0.4/2, \; 0.1/3\}$ (notice this is "NOT large" — in fact somewhat small), compute the implied $\tilde{A}'$ via GMT.

#### Step 1 — Take the Transpose of $R$

Since GMT uses $\tilde{R}^T$, we swap rows and columns:

$$R^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.2 & 0.2 \\ 0.6 & 0.6 & 0.2 \\ 1 & 0.6 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 2 — Apply Sup-Min Composition: $\tilde{A}' = \tilde{B}' \circ R^T$

For $x_1 = 1$ (column 1 of $R^T$):

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}'}(1) = \max\{\min(0.8, 0.2), \; \min(0.4, 0.6), \; \min(0.1, 1)\}$$
$$= \max\{0.2, \; 0.4, \; 0.1\} = 0.4$$

For $x_2 = 2$ (column 2 of $R^T$):

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}'}(2) = \max\{\min(0.8, 0.2), \; \min(0.4, 0.6), \; \min(0.1, 0.6)\}$$
$$= \max\{0.2, \; 0.4, \; 0.1\} = 0.4$$

For $x_3 = 3$ (column 3 of $R^T$):

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}'}(3) = \max\{\min(0.8, 0.2), \; \min(0.4, 0.2), \; \min(0.1, 0.2)\}$$
$$= \max\{0.2, \; 0.2, \; 0.1\} = 0.2$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\tilde{A}' = \{0.4/1, \; 0.4/2, \; 0.2/3\}}$$

#### Step 3 — Interpretation

Since $\tilde{B}'$ is "not large" (the output is small), the GMT concludes $X$ is "not particularly small" either — the conclusion $\tilde{A}'$ has **lower membership for small values** compared to $\tilde{A}$ itself. This reverse reasoning is precisely how diagnostic expert systems (e.g., medical AI) backtrack symptoms to probable causes.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — GMP vs. GMT Side-by-Side

| Aspect | Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP) | Generalized Modus Tollens (GMT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of Reasoning** | Forward (Antecedent → Consequent) | Backward (Consequent → Antecedent) |
| **Input Given** | $\tilde{A}'$ (new fact about $X$) | $\tilde{B}'$ (observed fact about $Y$) |
| **Output Computed** | $\tilde{B}'$ (conclusion about $Y$) | $\tilde{A}'$ (conclusion about $X$) |
| **Composition Used** | $\tilde{B}' = \tilde{A}' \circ \tilde{R}$ | $\tilde{A}' = \tilde{B}' \circ \tilde{R}^T$ |
| **Use Case** | Control systems (input → action) | Diagnosis (symptom → cause) |
| **Reduction to Classical** | When $\tilde{A}' = \tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{B}' = \tilde{B}$, reduces to classical Modus Ponens | When $\tilde{B}' = \overline{\tilde{B}}$, reduces to classical Modus Tollens |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow — Arithmetic Operations on Fuzzy Numbers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Two Fuzzy Numbers A and B] --> B[Step 1: Define Membership Functions]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Alpha-Cuts A_alpha and B_alpha as Closed Intervals]
    C --> D{Step 3: Select Arithmetic Operation}
    D --> D1[Addition: A plus B]
    D --> D2[Subtraction: A minus B]
    D --> D3[Multiplication: A times B]
    D --> D4[Division: A divided by B]
    D1 --> E1[Interval Sum: a1 plus b1, a2 plus b2]
    D2 --> E2[Interval Difference: a1 minus b2, a2 minus b1]
    D3 --> E3{Non-negative?}
    E3 -- Yes --> E3a[a1 times b1, a2 times b2]
    E3 -- No --> E3b[Min and Max over all 4 products]
    D4 --> E4{Positive?}
    E4 -- Yes --> E4a[a1 over b2, a2 over b1]
    E4 -- No --> E4b[Min and Max over all 4 quotients]
    E1 --> F[Step 4: Reconstruct Fuzzy Result via Resolution Identity]
    E2 --> F
    E3a --> F
    E3b --> F
    E4a --> F
    E4b --> F
    F --> G[Output: Resulting Fuzzy Number]

    style A fill:#e1f5e1
    style G fill:#e1e1f5
    style D fill:#fff4cc
    style E3 fill:#fff4cc
    style E4 fill:#fff4cc
```

### 4.2 GMP Inference Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Premise1[Premise 1: Fuzzy Rule]
        A1[Fuzzy Set A on X] --> B1[Implication Operator]
        B2[Fuzzy Set B on Y] --> B1
        B1 --> R[Fuzzy Relation R = A times B]
    end

    subgraph Premise2[Premise 2: New Fact]
        Ap[Fuzzy Set A prime on X]
    end

    R --> COMP[Sup-Min Composition]
    Ap --> COMP

    COMP --> Bp[Conclusion: Fuzzy Set B prime on Y]

    style A1 fill:#cce5ff
    style B2 fill:#cce5ff
    style Ap fill:#ffe5cc
    style Bp fill:#ccffcc
    style COMP fill:#ffcccc
```

### 4.3 GMT Reverse Reasoning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[Premise 1: Same Fuzzy Rule]
        A1b[Fuzzy Set A] --> Imp[Implication: R]
        B2b[Fuzzy Set B] --> Imp
        Imp --> Rb[Fuzzy Relation R]
    end

    subgraph P2[Premise 2: Observed Output]
        Bp2[Observed Fuzzy Set B prime on Y]
    end

    Rb --> TRAN[Transpose R to R transpose]
    TRAN --> COMP2[Sup-Min Composition]
    Bp2 --> COMP2

    COMP2 --> Ap2[Conclusion: Fuzzy Set A prime on X]

    style A1b fill:#cce5ff
    style B2b fill:#cce5ff
    style Bp2 fill:#ffe5cc
    style Ap2 fill:#ccffcc
    style TRAN fill:#ffcccc
    style COMP2 fill:#ffcccc
```

### 4.4 Comparison Map — Classical vs. Generalized Reasoning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Classical Modus Ponens: P implies Q, P, therefore Q] --> C2[Special Case: A prime equals A and B prime equals B]
    C3[Classical Modus Tollens: P implies Q, not Q, therefore not P] --> C4[Special Case: B prime equals complement of B]

    C2 --> GMP[GMP: A prime near A gives B prime near B]
    C4 --> GMT[GMT: B prime not equal B gives A prime not equal A]

    GMP --> APP1[Application: Fuzzy Controllers]
    GMT --> APP2[Application: Expert Diagnostic Systems]

```
