# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Architecture of Bayesian Inference in a Game

The following block diagram maps the *functional architecture* of how a rational player forms and uses beliefs within a Bayesian game. This is the cognitive/algorithmic flow of inference.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Nature Draws Type Profile t = t1...tn from Common Prior p] --> B[Player i Observes Own Type ti]
    B --> C[Form Type-Conditional Prior Belief p t_neg_i given t_i]
    C --> D{Static or Dynamic Game?}
    D -- Static --> E[Choose Action ai by Maximizing Expected Utility EU_i]
    D -- Dynamic --> F[Observe Opponent Action a_neg_i]
    F --> G[Bayesian Posterior Update: p t_j given t_i and a_neg_i]
    G --> E
    E --> H[Outcome Realization: Payoffs u_i a, t]
    H --> I{Equilibrium Conditions Satisfied?}
    I -- No --> J[Deviate: Re-Optimize]
    J --> C
    I -- Yes --> K[Bayesian Nash Equilibrium / Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium]
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram: Interaction Between Sender and Receiver in a Signaling Game

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant N as Nature
    participant S as Sender (informed player)
    participant R as Receiver (uninformed player)
    N->>S: Draw type theta from p
    Note over S: S knows theta
    S->>R: Send signal m in M
    Note over R: R updates belief mu(theta given m) via Bayes' Rule
    R->>R: Choose action a to maximize EU_R
    R-->>S: Action a realized
    Note over S,R: Equilibrium when S's signal is optimal given R's best response,<br/>and R's belief is consistent with S's strategy
```

## 4.3 Concept Map: Equilibrium Concepts Built on Inference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Games with Complete Information] --> B[Nash Equilibrium NE]
    C[Games with Incomplete Information] --> D[Bayesian Nash Equilibrium BNE]
    C --> E[Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium PBE]
    C --> F[Sequential Equilibrium]
    C --> G[Trembling Hand Perfect Equilibrium]
    B --> H[Subgame Perfect Nash Equilibrium]
    H --> F
    D --> E
    E --> F
    style A fill:#E8F4FD
    style C fill:#FFF4E6
    style D fill:#D4EDDA
    style E fill:#F8D7DA
    style F fill:#F8D7DA
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: A Complete Bayesian Game with Three Information Sets

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Game Start: Nature Draws Type t in TL, TH] -->|Probability q| HL[Type theta_H High]
    Start -->|Probability 1-q| LL[Type theta_L Low]
    HL --> S1[Player 1 Chooses m1 or m2]
    LL --> S2[Player 1 Chooses m1 or m2]
    S1 -->|m1 chosen| R1[Receiver's Info Set I_m1]
    S1 -->|m2 chosen| R2[Receiver's Info Set I_m2]
    S2 -->|m1 chosen| R1
    S2 -->|m2 chosen| R2
    R1 --> A1[Receiver Chooses a1]
    R1 --> A2[Receiver Chooses a2]
    R2 --> A3[Receiver Chooses a1]
    R2 --> A4[Receiver Chooses a2]
    A1 --> Pay1[Payoff u1, uR]
    A2 --> Pay2[Payoff u1, uR]
    A3 --> Pay3[Payoff u1, uR]
    A4 --> Pay4[Payoff u1, uR]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Structural Observation:** Both types of Player 1 land in the *same* information set $I_{m_1}$ for the Receiver. The Receiver must therefore hold a *belief distribution* (a probability over types) at this information set. This is the *fundamental informational challenge* that inference resolves.

## 4.5 Schematic: The Revelation Principle Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Indirect Mechanism Gamma] --> B[Players Have Private Types]
    B --> C[Equilibrium Strategy s*]
    C --> D[Outcome O]
    E[Direct Revelation Mechanism Gamma'] --> F[Players Report Types t hat]
    F --> G[Truthful Equilibrium t hat = t]
    G --> H[Same Outcome O]
    style A fill:#FFF3CD
    style E fill:#D4EDDA
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**Define a Bayesian game. Explain the common prior assumption with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Bayesian game** is a strategic-form game with incomplete information, formally defined as the tuple
$$G = \big(N, \{A_i\}_{i \in N}, \{T_i\}_{i \in N}, \{u_i\}_{i \in N}, p\big)$$
where $N$ is the set of players, $A_i$ is player $i$'s action set, $T_i$ is player $i$'s private type set, $u_i$ is player $i$'s utility function depending on actions and types, and $p$ is the common prior probability distribution over the type profile space $T = \prod_i T_i$.

The **Common Prior Assumption (CPA)** states that all players share the same probability distribution $p$ over types, and they all know that this distribution is shared. Each player, after observing their own type $t_i$, conditions on it to form beliefs about others:
$$p(t_{-i} \mid t_i) = \frac{p(t_i, t_{-i})}{\sum_{t_{-i}'} p(t_i, t_{-i}')}$$

**Example:** In a first-price auction, each bidder's valuation is their private type, and the prior distribution over valuations is the common prior that every bidder uses to form beliefs about competitors.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**What is a Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium (PBE)? State the two conditions it must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium (PBE)** is a solution concept for extensive-form games with incomplete information. It consists of a pair $(s^*, \mu)$ where $s^*$ is a profile of behavioral strategies and $\mu$ is a profile of belief systems over types at each information set.

The two conditions are:

1. **Sequential Rationality:** At every information set $I_i$, given belief $\mu_i(I_i)$ and the continuation strategies $s_{-i}^*$, the strategy $s_i^*$ at $I_i$ maximizes player $i$'s expected utility.

$$\forall I_i, \;\; s_i^*(\cdot \mid I_i) \in \arg\max_{a_i \in A_i(I_i)} \mathbb{E}\Big[\,u_i \,\Big|\, I_i,\; \mu_i(I_i),\; s_{-i}^*\,\Big]$$

2. **Belief Consistency:** On the **equilibrium path** (i.e., for histories reached with positive probability under $s^*$ and $p$), beliefs are obtained from $s^*$ and $p$ via Bayes' rule. Off-equilibrium beliefs may be specified freely but must respect certain refinements (e.g., Kreps-Wilson).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample QP 2024 Scheme]

**A Bayesian Cournot Duopoly.** Two firms, Firm 1 and Firm 2, produce a homogeneous good. Inverse demand is $P(Q) = 120 - Q$ where $Q = q_1 + q_2$. Firm 2's marginal cost $c_2$ is its private type, taking values $c_2 \in \{20, 60\}$ with equal probability. Firm 1's marginal cost is common knowledge at $c_1 = 40$.

**(a)** Write down the formal structure of this Bayesian game. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the Bayesian Nash Equilibrium quantities $q_1^*$, $q_2^*(c_2=20)$, and $q_2^*(c_2=60)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Game Structure (7 marks):**

The Bayesian game is the tuple $G = (N, \{A_i\}, \{T_i\}, \{u_i\}, p)$ with:

- **Players:** $N = \{1, 2\}$
- **Action sets:** $A_1 = A_2 = [0, 120]$ (quantities chosen)
- **Type set for player 2:** $T_2 = \{20, 60\}$ (private marginal cost of Firm 2)
- **Type set for player 1:** $T_1 = \{40\}$ (degenerate — known cost)
- **Common prior:** $p(c_2 = 20) = p(c_2 = 60) = 0.5$
- **Utility (profit) functions:**

$$u_1(q_1, q_2) = (120 - q_1 - q_2) q_1 - 40 q_1$$

$$u_2(q_1, q_2, c_2) = (120 - q_1 - q_2) q_2 - c_2 q_2$$

Firm 1 does not know $c_2$ but holds the belief $p(c_2 = 20) = p(c_2 = 60) = 0.5$.

**Part (b) — Computing the BNE (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Firm 2's profit maximization for each type. FOC for Firm 2:

$$\frac{\partial \pi_2}{\partial q_2} = 120 - q_1 - 2q_2 - c_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad q_2^*(c_2) = \frac{120 - q_1 - c_2}{2}$$

Thus:
$$q_2^*(c_2 = 20) = \frac{100 - q_1}{2}, \quad q_2^*(c_2 = 60) = \frac{60 - q_1}{2}$$

**Step 2:** Firm 1's expected profit, averaging over its belief about $c_2$:

$$\mathbb{E}[\pi_1] = 0.5 \cdot \big[(120 - q_1 - q_2^*(20)) q_1 - 40 q_1\big] + 0.5 \cdot \big[(120 - q_1 - q_2^*(60)) q_1 - 40 q_1\big]$$

Substituting:

$$\mathbb{E}[\pi_1] = 0.5 q_1 \left[80 - q_1 - \frac{100 - q_1}{2}\right] + 0.5 q_1 \left[80 - q_1 - \frac{60 - q_1}{2}\right]$$

**Step 3:** Simplify each bracket. First bracket:

$$80 - q_1 - 50 + \frac{q_1}{2} = 30 - \frac{q_1}{2}$$

Second bracket:

$$80 - q_1 - 30 + \frac{q_1}{2} = 50 - \frac{q_1}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Combine:

$$\mathbb{E}[\pi_1] = 0.5 q_1 \left(30 - \frac{q_1}{2}\right) + 0.5 q_1 \left(50 - \frac{q_1}{2}\right) = q_1 (40 - \frac{q_1}{2})$$

**Step 5:** FOC for Firm 1:

$$\frac{d \mathbb{E}[\pi_1]}{d q_1} = 40 - q_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad q_1^* = 40$$

**Step 6:** Plug back into Firm 2's best responses:

$$q_2^*(c_2 = 20) = \frac{100 - 40}{2} = 30$$

$$q_2^*(c_2 = 60) = \frac{60 - 40}{2} = 10$$

**Equilibrium:** $q_1^* = 40$, $q_2^*(20) = 30$, $q_2^*(60) = 10$. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample QP 2024 Scheme]

**A Signaling Game (Spence's Education Model).** Consider a job market with one worker (Sender) and one firm (Receiver). The worker's productivity type is $\theta \in \{L, H\}$ where $L = \text{Low}$, $H = \text{High}$, with prior $p(H) = 0.3$. The worker can acquire education $e \in \{e_L, e_H\}$ (a signal) at cost $c(\theta, e)$ given by the table:

| Type | Cost of $e_L$ | Cost of $e_H$ |
|---|---|---|
| Low ($\theta_L$) | 0 | 30 |
| High ($\theta_H$) | 0 | 10 |

The firm offers a wage $w(e) \in \{w_L, w_H\}$ with $w_L = 50$, $w_H = 100$. Payoffs: worker gets $w - c$; firm gets $\theta - w$.

**(a)** Identify all *pooling* and *separating* Perfect Bayesian Equilibria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of the **Intuitive Criterion** (Cho-Kreps) as a refinement. Which of your equilibria in (a) survive this criterion? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Finding PBE (7 marks):**

**Separating Equilibrium candidate:** High types choose $e_H$, Low types choose $e_L$.

- *Low type:* Gets $w_L = 50$ (firm's belief: $\mu(H \mid e_L) = 0$). Deviation to $e_H$ yields $100 - 30 = 70 > 50$. So **Low type deviates**! Separating equilibrium fails under this parameterization.

**Try other separating candidate:** Low types choose $e_H$, High types choose $e_L$.

- This is not credible because High types have lower cost of $e_H$ — they would deviate.

**Pooling Equilibrium candidate:** Both types choose $e_H$.

- Firm's belief: $\mu(H \mid e_H) = 0.3$. Firm's best response: $w(e_H) = 100$ if $0.3 \cdot 100 + 0.7 \cdot 100 \geq 0.3 \cdot 50 + 0.7 \cdot 50$? Firm's payoff from $w_H$: $0.3 \cdot (H - 100) + 0.7 \cdot (L - 100) = 0.3 H + 0.7 L - 100$. From $w_L$: $0.3 H + 0.7 L - 50$. So $w_L$ gives higher payoff → firm offers $w_L = 50$.
- Worker of type $H$: Deviation to $e_L$ yields $50 - 0 = 50$ (since firm offers $w_L$ anyway on equilibrium path? No — but off-path beliefs matter.)

For a **pooling at $e_L$** equilibrium: both types choose $e_L$, firm infers nothing → $\mu(H \mid e_L) = 0.3$, offers $w_H = 100$ (since expected productivity $0.3 H + 0.7 L$ exceeds wage).
- Deviation: High type deviating to $e_H$ yields $50 - 10 = 40 < 100$. No deviation.

→ **Pooling at $e_L$ is a PBE** when $100 - 0 \geq 50 - 10$, i.e., always for high types, and $100 - 0 \geq 50 - 30$, i.e., always for low types. ✓

**Part (b) — Intuitive Criterion (7 marks):**

The **Intuitive Criterion** (Cho-Kreps 1987) eliminates "unreasonable" off-equilibrium beliefs. A PBE fails the Intuitive Criterion if there exists an off-equilibrium message $m$ such that the equilibrium off-path belief assigns positive probability to types for whom $m$ is *dominated* (i.e., strictly worse than some other action for *every* belief in the support of the off-path belief).

For the **pooling at $e_L$** PBE: Off-path message is $e_H$. The Intuitive Criterion asks: would *any* type of worker strictly prefer $e_H$ over $e_L$ for *every* possible firm response? 
- A High type gets $w - 10$ from $e_H$ and $w - 0$ from $e_L$. Even if firm offers $w_H = 100$ for $e_H$, High type gets $90$ vs. $50$ for $e_L$. So High type would *always* prefer $e_H$.
- A Low type gets $w - 30$ from $e_H$ and $w - 0$ from $e_L$. If firm offers $w_L = 50$ for $e_H$, Low type gets $20$ vs. $50$ for $e_L$. So Low type would *not* always prefer $e_H$.

The off-path belief $\mu(H \mid e_H)$ should place probability 1 on the type that *uniquely* benefits from deviation. By the Intuitive Criterion, the only consistent belief is $\mu(H \mid e_H) = 1$, so the firm offers $w_H = 100$. But then Low type's *deviation payoff* ($100 - 30 = 70$) exceeds their *equilibrium payoff* ($100 - 0 = 100$)... wait, $70 < 100$, so no deviation. The Intuitive Criterion refines beliefs but the pooling PBE **survives** the Intuitive Criterion here.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors (Valuation Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Forgetting to condition on own type:** Beliefs $p(t_{-i} \mid t_i)$ must be derived from the common prior by conditioning. Students often use $p(t_{-i})$ directly — this is a fundamental error worth **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Off-path belief errors:** Many PBE problems have multiple equilibria. Students who specify *arbitrary* off-path beliefs without checking sequential rationality lose **1.5–2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up BNE and PBE:** A BNE is for static Bayesian games; a PBE is for dynamic games. Confusing them is a **1-mark conceptual deduction**.
> 4. **Skipping the FOC sign check:** A best-response FOC can be a maximum or minimum. Students must verify the second-order condition (concavity of $EU_i$ in $a_i$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Inference | 30-Second Revision Bullets**

- **Bayesian Game tuple** $G = (N, A_i, T_i, u_i, p)$ — memorize the 5 components.
- **Harsanyi Transformation** introduces Nature as a fictitious player to convert incomplete information to imperfect information.
- **Common Prior Assumption (CPA)** — all players share the same $p$ and know that they share it.
- **Type-conditional beliefs** are derived by conditioning the common prior on one's own type.
- **BNE condition:** $s_i^*(t_i) \in \arg\max_{a_i} \mathbb{E}_{t_{-i}|t_i}[u_i(a_i, s_{-i}^*(t_{-i}), t)]$ for every $i, t_i$.
- **Bayes' Rule** governs dynamic updating after observing an opponent's action.
- **PBE** = Sequential Rationality **+** Belief Consistency.
- **Equilibrium path beliefs** = strict application of Bayes' Rule; **off-path beliefs** constrained by refinements (Intuitive Criterion, D1, Divinity, etc.).
- **Single-Crossing Property (SCP)** — sufficient condition for monotone pure-strategy BNE.
- **Revelation Principle** — any indirect mechanism's equilibrium outcome can be replicated by a direct truthful mechanism.
- **Pooling vs. Separating Equilibria** — pooling = same action for all types; separating = different actions.
- **Worked formula patterns** to memorize:
  - $EU_i = \sum_{t_{-i}} p(t_{-i} \mid t_i) \cdot u_i(\cdot)$
  - $\mu(\theta \mid m) = \frac{p(\theta) \mathbb{P}(m \mid \theta)}{\sum_{\theta'} p(\theta') \mathbb{P}(m \mid \theta')}$
- **Real-world linkages** — auctions (4G/5G spectrum), insurance markets, smart-grid demand response, online ad auctions, signaling in labor markets.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always (1) write the formal game structure, (2) derive the type-conditional belief explicitly, (3) write the expected utility, (4) compute the FOC and verify, (5) check second-order condition, and (6) state the equilibrium in clean form. This 6-step recipe earns full marks consistently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rank Ordering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST753 - GAME THEORY AND MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rank Ordering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Rank Ordering** is a foundational concept in *Social Choice Theory* and *Mechanism Design* in which each agent $i \in N = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ reports a **complete (or partial) ordinal ranking** $\succsim_i$ over a finite set of alternatives $A = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_m\}$ rather than reporting a numerical (cardinal) utility value $u_i(a)$.

Formally, a **Rank-Order Mechanism (or Social Choice Function, SCF)** is a mapping:

$$ f: \mathcal{L}^n \longrightarrow A $$

where $\mathcal{L}$ is the set of all linear orders (permutations / total preorders) over $A$, and the output $a^\star = f(\succsim_1, \succsim_2, \ldots, \succsim_n)$ is the socially chosen alternative.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ordinal vs Cardinal Distinction (KTU High-Yield)**
> - **Ordinal preference**: Only the *order* matters. We know that $a \succsim_i b$, but NOT *how much* better $a$ is than $b$.
> - **Cardinal preference**: The actual utility magnitudes $u_i(a)$ are reported or known.
> - Rank Ordering uses **ordinal** information only. This is realistic because real agents rarely know their own exact utility numbers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Election Booth"

Imagine a college election with **3 candidates**: Ashwin (A), Bhavya (B), and Chandan (C). Each voter walks into a booth and is given a **ballot paper**, not a "utility meter". The voter simply writes:

> 1. A
> 2. B
> 3. C

This ballot is the voter's **rank-order report**. The ballot box aggregates all such rankings, and a **voting rule** (Plurality, Borda, Runoff, etc.) selects the winner.

**Key insight**: The voter reports *no numbers*. There is no "I love Ashwin 87 units and Bhavya 42 units." The system only sees the order. This is the essence of **Rank Ordering**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why rank-order mechanisms dominate real-world design**: 
> - Easier to elicit (people know their preferences, not their utility magnitudes)
> - Less incentive to misreport (because there is no numerical exaggeration possible)
> - Naturally handles **single-peaked** or **single-crossing** preference structures

## 1.3 Scope in KTU Module 3

In Module 3 of PECST753, Rank Ordering typically covers:

| Sub-topic | KTU Significance |
|---|---|
| Voting Rules (Plurality, Borda, Veto, Runoff) | ⭐⭐⭐ Frequently asked |
| Gibbard–Satterthwaite Theorem | ⭐⭐⭐ Theorem-based question |
| Arrow's Impossibility Theorem | ⭐⭐ Most popular question |
| Serial Dictatorship | ⭐⭐⭐ Algorithm + strategy-proofness |
| Top Trading Cycles (TTC) | ⭐⭐ Application question |
| Single-Peaked Preferences | ⭐⭐ Mediator agent model |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Framework

Let us formalize the players, preferences, and outcomes:

- **Agent set**: $N = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$
- **Alternative set**: $A = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_m\}$, with $\vert A \vert = m \geq 3$
- **Preference profile of agent $i$**: $R_i$ is a complete, transitive, antisymmetric binary relation on $A$
- **Profile vector**: $R = (R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n)$
- **Social Choice Function (SCF)**: $f: \mathcal{R}^n \to A$ where $\mathcal{R}$ is the set of all preferences
- **Social Welfare Function (SWF)**: $F: \mathcal{R}^n \to \mathcal{R}$ (outputs a *social ranking*, not just a winner)

## 2.2 The Five Core Properties of Rank-Order Mechanisms

A SCF $f$ may or may not satisfy the following **KTU-mandated properties**:

1. **Strategy-Proofness (SP)** / Dominant Strategy Incentive Compatibility (DSIC):
   $$\forall\, i, \forall\, R_i, R_i', R_{-i} : \quad f(R_i, R_{-i}) \succsim_i f(R_i', R_{-i})$$
   *No agent can ever benefit by misreporting their true ranking, regardless of others.*

2. **Pareto Efficiency (PE) / Unanimity**: If every agent ranks $a$ above $b$, then society must rank $a$ above $b$:
   $$[\forall i : a \succ_i b] \implies [F(R)(a) \succ F(R)(b)]$$

3. **Independence of Irrelevant Alternatives (IIA)**: The social ranking between any two alternatives depends *only* on the individual rankings between those two:
   $$[\forall i : R_i \vert_{\{a,b\}} = R_i' \vert_{\{a,b\}}] \implies [F(R)(a) \succ F(R)(b) \iff F(R')(a) \succ F(R')(b)]$$

4. **Non-Dictatorship**: No single agent $d$ exists such that:
   $$f(R) = \text{argmax}_a \in A\; R_d(a) \quad \forall R$$

5. **Anonymity & Neutrality**: The rule treats agents symmetrically and alternatives symmetrically.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Takeaway**: Strategy-proofness + onto ($f$ can produce any alternative) + $\geq 3$ alternatives ⇒ **dictatorship**. This is the Gibbard–Satterthwaite Theorem. Arrow's theorem is the parallel result for *social welfare functions* (SWFs) with four axioms.

## 2.3 The Five Most Important Rank-Order Voting Rules

### Rule 1: Plurality Rule
Each voter votes for their **most-preferred** alternative. The candidate with the most first-place votes wins.

$$\text{Score}_P(a) = \vert \{ i : a \text{ is top of } R_i \} \vert$$

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in A} \text{Score}_P(a)$$

### Rule 2: Borda Count
Each alternative receives points based on its rank position. With $m$ alternatives:

$$\text{Score}_B(a) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bigl( m - \text{rank}_i(a) \bigr)$$

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in A} \text{Score}_B(a)$$

- 1st place: $m - 1$ points
- 2nd place: $m - 2$ points
- ...
- Last place: $0$ points

### Rule 3: Plurality with Runoff
1. Top two candidates by Plurality advance.
2. Head-to-head majority vote between them decides the winner.

### Rule 4: Veto (Anti-Plurality)
Each voter *eliminates* their least-preferred alternative. The alternative with **fewest** last-place votes wins (i.e., with highest "survival count"):

$$\text{Score}_V(a) = \vert \{ i : a \text{ is NOT bottom of } R_i \} \vert$$

### Rule 5: Condorcet Winner (Pairwise Majority)
Alternative $a$ is the Condorcet winner if it defeats every other alternative in pairwise majority contests:

$$\forall\, b \in A \setminus \{a\}: \quad \vert \{ i : a \succ_i b \} \vert > \vert \{ i : b \succ_i a \} \vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condorcet Paradox**: A Condorcet winner may fail to *exist* due to majority cycles: $A \succ B \succ C \succ A$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

| Rule / Property | Formula | Output Type | Strategy-Proof? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plurality | $\text{Score}_P(a) = \sum_i \mathbb{1}[a = \text{top}(R_i)]$ | Single winner | ❌ No | Vulnerable to "vote-splitting" |
| Borda Count | $\text{Score}_B(a) = \sum_i (m - \text{rank}_i(a))$ | Single winner | ❌ No | Most "fair" cardinal-style ordinal |
| Veto | $\text{Score}_V(a) = \sum_i \mathbb{1}[a \neq \text{bottom}(R_i)]$ | Single winner | ❌ No | Symmetric dual of Plurality |
| Plurality Runoff | Top 2 + head-to-head | Single winner | ❌ No | Used in French Presidential elections |
| Condorcet | $\forall b: \vert\{i:a \succ_i b\}\vert > \vert\{i:b \succ_i a\}\vert$ | Single winner | ❌ No (but Paretian if exists) | May fail to exist (cycles) |
| Serial Dictatorship | $\pi(1)$ picks first, $\pi(2)$ picks from rest, … | Allocation | ✅ **Yes** | Strategy-proof allocation |
| Top Trading Cycle | Graph cycle removal | Allocation | ✅ **Yes** | Used in NYC school choice |
| Dictatorship | $f(R) = \text{top}(R_d)$ | Single winner | ✅ **Yes** | Trivial, but Gibbard–Satterthwaite forces this |

## 2.5 Strategy-Proof Rank-Order Mechanisms

### Serial Dictatorship (SD)

Given an exogenous priority order $\pi = (\pi(1), \pi(2), \ldots, \pi(n))$, agents pick in turn from the remaining set:

$$\text{Step } k: \quad \text{agent } \pi(k) \text{ picks } \arg\max_{a \in A \setminus \text{picked}} R_{\pi(k)}(a)$$

This is the canonical **strategy-proof** rank-order allocation mechanism.

### Top Trading Cycles (TTC)

1. Each remaining agent points an arrow at the most-preferred unallocated house (alternative).
2. Find a directed cycle in this graph.
3. Execute the cycle trade (each agent in the cycle gets the house they pointed to).
4. Remove traded agents and houses; repeat until all are matched.

**Both SD and TTC are dominant-strategy incentive compatible.**

## 2.6 Engineering / Real-World Utility

- **School Choice (NYC, Boston, Chennai EWS)**: TTC algorithm
- **Medical Residency Matching (NRMP, India PG)**: SD with Gale-Shapley
- **Spectrum Auctions (FCC)**: Rank-order bidding formats
- **Social Welfare Programs**: Borda-based poverty ranking
- **Multi-criteria Decision Making (MCDM) in Engineering**: Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) — direct descendant of rank-ordering

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Borda Count Computation

**Problem**: 5 voters, 4 candidates $\{A, B, C, D\}$. Ballots:
- Voter 1: $A \succ B \succ C \succ D$
- Voter 2: $B \succ A \succ C \succ D$
- Voter 3: $A \succ B \succ D \succ C$
- Voter 4: $C \succ D \succ A \succ B$
- Voter 5: $D \succ C \succ B \succ A$

Here $m = 4$, so points are 3, 2, 1, 0.

**Step 1**: Assign Borda points to each voter-ranking.

| Voter | 1st (3 pts) | 2nd (2 pts) | 3rd (1 pt) | 4th (0 pts) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | B | C | D |
| 2 | B | A | C | D |
| 3 | A | B | D | C |
| 4 | C | D | A | B |
| 5 | D | C | B | A |

**Step 2**: Tally scores.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_B(A) &= 3 + 0 + 3 + 1 + 0 = 7 \\
\text{Score}_B(B) &= 2 + 3 + 2 + 0 + 1 = 8 \\
\text{Score}_B(C) &= 1 + 1 + 0 + 3 + 2 = 7 \\
\text{Score}_B(D) &= 0 + 0 + 1 + 2 + 3 = 6
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3**: Declare the winner.

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a} \text{Score}_B(a) = B \quad \text{(with 8 points)}$$

**Conclusion**: Candidate **B** wins under Borda Count. (Note: A would have won under Plurality with 2 first-place votes versus B's 1.)

## 3.2 Worked Example — Condorcet Paradox

**Problem**: 3 voters, 3 alternatives $\{A, B, C\}$.
- Voter 1: $A \succ B \succ C$
- Voter 2: $B \succ C \succ A$
- Voter 3: $C \succ A \succ B$

**Step 1**: Pairwise contest $A$ vs $B$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Votes for A over B} &: 2 \text{ (Voters 1, 3)} \\
\text{Votes for B over A} &: 1 \text{ (Voter 2)}
\end{aligned}$$

$A \succ_{M} B$ ✓ (majority).

**Step 2**: Pairwise contest $B$ vs $C$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Votes for B over C} &: 2 \text{ (Voters 1, 2)} \\
\text{Votes for C over B} &: 1 \text{ (Voter 3)}
\end{aligned}$$

$B \succ_{M} C$ ✓ (majority).

**Step 3**: Pairwise contest $C$ vs $A$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Votes for C over A} &: 2 \text{ (Voters 2, 3)} \\
\text{Votes for A over C} &: 1 \text{ (Voter 1)}
\end{aligned}$$

$C \succ_{M} A$ ✓ (majority).

**Step 4**: Detect cycle.

$$A \succ_{M} B \succ_{M} C \succ_{M} A \quad \text{(cyclic majority)}$$

**Conclusion**: No Condorcet winner exists — the **Condorcet Paradox** is realized. The majority preference is *intransitive*.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Plurality with Runoff

**Problem**: 7 voters, 3 candidates.
- 3 voters: $A \succ B \succ C$
- 2 voters: $B \succ C \succ A$
- 2 voters: $C \succ A \succ B$

**Step 1**: Round 1 — Count first-place votes.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_P(A) &= 3 \\
\text{Score}_P(B) &= 2 \\
\text{Score}_P(C) &= 2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2**: Top two advance. The two top first-place getters are **A** (3) and tie between B and C (2 each). Break tie by coin toss — say $B$ advances.

**Step 3**: Round 2 — Pairwise contest A vs B among all 7 voters.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Votes for A over B} &: 3 + 2 = 5 \quad (\text{Voters 1-3 + Voters 6-7}) \\
\text{Votes for B over A} &: 2 + 0 = 2 \quad (\text{Voters 4-5})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4**: Declare winner.

$$a^* = A \quad (5 \text{ vs } 2 \text{ votes})$$

## 3.4 Serial Dictatorship — Allocation Worked Example

**Problem**: 3 agents, 3 indivisible goods $\{h_1, h_2, h_3\}$, priority order $\pi = (1, 2, 3)$.

Preferences:
- Agent 1: $h_1 \succ h_2 \succ h_3$
- Agent 2: $h_2 \succ h_3 \succ h_1$
- Agent 3: $h_3 \succ h_1 \succ h_2$

**Step 1**: Agent $\pi(1) = 1$ picks first from $A = \{h_1, h_2, h_3\}$.

$$\arg\max R_1 = h_1 \implies \text{Agent 1 gets } h_1$$

Remaining: $\{h_2, h_3\}$.

**Step 2**: Agent $\pi(2) = 2$ picks from $\{h_2, h_3\}$.

$$\arg\max_{a \in \{h_2, h_3\}} R_2(a) = h_2 \implies \text{Agent 2 gets } h_2$$

Remaining: $\{h_3\}$.

**Step 3**: Agent $\pi(3) = 3$ gets the last.

$$\text{Agent 3 gets } h_3$$

**Final Allocation**: Agent 1 → $h_1$, Agent 2 → $h_2$, Agent 3 → $h_3$. Each agent gets their top choice ⇒ **Pareto optimal**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategy-proofness verification of SD**: Because agent $\pi(k)$ picks from the *remaining* set after agents ahead of them have removed their preferred items, misreporting $R_{\pi(k)}$ cannot give a strictly better outcome — there is no swap possible since later agents cannot return items. Hence SD is **DSIC**.

## 3.5 Top Trading Cycles (TTC) — Worked Example

**Problem**: 3 agents, 3 houses (same preferences as above).

$$h_1 \succ_1 h_2 \succ_1 h_3 \quad ; \quad h_2 \succ_2 h_3 \succ_2 h_1 \quad ; \quad h_3 \succ_3 h_1 \succ_3 h_2$$

**Step 1**: Each agent points to their **most-preferred remaining** house.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Agent 1} &\to h_1 \\
\text{Agent 2} &\to h_2 \\
\text{Agent 3} &\to h_3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2**: Detect cycle.

$$1 \to h_1 \quad ; \quad 2 \to h_2 \quad ; \quad 3 \to h_3$$

Each house currently occupied by the agent who wants it — trivially a cycle: $1 \to h_1 \to 1$.

In non-trivial cases, draw a directed graph where agents point to occupants.

**Step 3**: Execute trade. Agent 1 gets $h_1$, Agent 2 gets $h_2$, Agent 3 gets $h_3$.

**Step 4**: All agents/houses matched → stop.

**Result**: Pareto-optimal allocation, identical to SD outcome here.

## 3.6 Sketch: Gibbard–Satterthwaite Theorem Proof Logic

**Statement**: Let $f: \mathcal{L}^n \to A$ be a SCF where $\vert A \vert \geq 3$ and $f$ is **onto** (every alternative can win for some profile) and **strategy-proof**. Then $f$ is **dictatorial**.

**Proof sketch (the standard KTU outline)**:

1. **Step 1**: Show $f$ is monotonic. If $a$ is the winner at $R$, and another profile $R'$ agrees with $R$ everywhere except that some agents *promote* $a$ higher, then $a$ is still the winner at $R'$.

$$\text{If } f(R) = a \text{ and } R_i'(a) \succsim_i R_i(a) \ \forall i, \text{ then } f(R') = a$$

2. **Step 2**: Show $f$ is *pairwise-dictatorial* on every pair. For any pair $\{a, b\}$, some agent $d$ is decisive between $a$ and $b$:

$$\forall i \neq d: [R_i(a) \succ_i R_i(b) \iff R_i'(a) \succ_i R_i'(b)] \implies f(R) = a \iff f(R') = a$$

3. **Step 3**: Show the same agent $d$ is decisive on *all* pairs. This is the heart of the theorem.

4. **Step 4**: Conclude $d$ is a global dictator:

$$\forall R: f(R) = \text{top}(R_d)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight**: Students often confuse Gibbard–Satterthwaite (GS) with Arrow's. **GS is for social choice functions (output is single alternative)**. **Arrow's is for social welfare functions (output is full ranking)**. Both have dictatorship as the only escape from impossibility.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rank-Order Mechanism Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Agent 1 Preference R1] --> D[Profile Vector R]
    B[Agent 2 Preference R2] --> D
    C[Agent N Preference RN] --> D
    D --> E{Voting Rule f}
    E -->|Plurality| F1[Top First-Place Count]
    E -->|Borda| F2[Weighted Rank Sum]
    E -->|Condorcet| F3[Pairwise Majority Contest]
    E -->|Runoff| F4[Two-Stage Elimination]
    F1 --> G[Winning Alternative a*]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Implementation / Allocation]
```

## 4.2 Strategy-Proofness Spectrum of Rank-Order Rules

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rank Order Rules] --> B[Strategy Proof]
    A --> C[Not Strategy Proof]
    B --> D[Dictatorship]
    B --> E[Serial Dictatorship]
    B --> F[Top Trading Cycles]
    B --> G[Random Dictatorship]
    C --> H[Plurality]
    C --> I[Borda Count]
    C --> J[Veto]
    C --> K[Plurality Runoff]
    C --> L[Condorcet if exists]
```

## 4.3 Top Trading Cycles (TTC) Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start with all agents and houses] --> P[Each agent points to most preferred remaining house]
    P --> Q{Directed graph formed}
    Q --> R[Find a directed cycle]
    R --> T[Execute trade within cycle]
    T --> U[Remove cycled agents and houses]
    U --> V{Any agents left?}
    V -->|Yes| P
    V -->|No| W[Output final allocation]
```

## 4.4 Condorcet Paradox Cycle Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Alternative A] -->|majority| B1[Alternative B]
    B1 -->|majority| C1[Alternative C]
    C1 -->|majority| A1
```

The cyclic structure $A \succ_M B \succ_M C \succ_M A$ demonstrates that majority preference aggregation over rankings can produce **intransitive social choices**, the root cause of both Arrow's and Gibbard–Satterthwaite impossibility results.

## 4.5 Serial Dictatorship Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Round 1 Agent pi of 1 picks top from full set] --> B0[Round 2 Agent pi of 2 picks top from remaining]
    B0 --> C0[Round 3 Agent pi of 3 picks top from remaining]
    C0 --> D0[Continue until all agents served]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between ordinal and cardinal preferences in the context of rank-order mechanisms. Why are rank-order mechanisms more commonly used in real-world voting systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

- **Ordinal preference (1 mark)**: Ranks alternatives in order without quantifying the intensity of preference. E.g., $A \succ B \succ C$.
- **Cardinal preference (1 mark)**: Specifies utility magnitudes, e.g., $u(A) = 90, u(B) = 50, u(C) = 10$.
- **Why more common (1 mark)**: Real agents know their orderings but rarely know exact utility magnitudes; ordinal reporting reduces strategic exaggeration (insincere voting) and is easier to elicit on ballots.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a Condorcet winner? Explain why a Condorcet winner may not exist in some preference profiles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

- **Definition (1.5 marks)**: A Condorcet winner is an alternative $a^\star$ that defeats every other alternative in a pairwise majority contest, i.e., for all $b \neq a^\star$, the number of voters preferring $a^\star$ to $b$ is strictly greater than those preferring $b$ to $a^\star$.
- **Non-existence (1.5 marks)**: Due to the **Condorcet Paradox** (cyclic majorities). When the majority relation is intransitive, e.g., $A \succ_M B \succ_M C \succ_M A$, no single alternative beats all others pairwise. Example: 3 voters with cyclic individual preferences produce a cyclic social preference.

---

## PART B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Voting Rules & Borda Analysis

**(a)** Define a Social Choice Function. Explain the **Plurality Rule**, **Borda Count**, and **Plurality with Runoff** as three examples of rank-order social choice functions. Compare their **strategy-proofness** properties. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Consider 9 voters and 4 candidates $\{P, Q, R, S\}$ with the following ballots:
- 3 voters: $P \succ Q \succ R \succ S$
- 2 voters: $Q \succ R \succ S \succ P$
- 2 voters: $R \succ S \succ P \succ Q$
- 2 voters: $S \succ P \succ Q \succ R$

Compute the winner under (i) Plurality, (ii) Borda Count, and (iii) Plurality with Runoff. Comment on why different rules give different outcomes. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A **Social Choice Function (SCF)** $f: \mathcal{R}^n \to A$ maps each profile of individual ordinal preferences into a single chosen alternative from $A$.

**Plurality Rule** (1.5 marks): Each voter names their single most-preferred candidate. The candidate with the highest tally wins. Formula:

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in A} \vert \{i : a = \text{top}(R_i)\} \vert$$

**Borda Count** (1.5 marks): Each candidate receives points $m - \text{rank}_i(a)$. Highest total wins:

$$a^* = \arg\max_a \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bigl(m - \text{rank}_i(a)\bigr)$$

**Plurality with Runoff** (1.5 marks): Round 1 — top two by plurality advance. Round 2 — head-to-head majority decides.

**Strategy-proofness comparison** (2.5 marks):
- **None of the three** is strategy-proof in general. All are vulnerable to **insincere voting**.
- Plurality invites *compromise* and *vote-splitting* strategies.
- Borda invites *burying* (ranking a strong rival last).
- Runoff invites *strategic entry* (backing a weak candidate to enter the runoff).
- Compare with **Serial Dictatorship** and **TTC**, which ARE strategy-proof.

#### Model Solution for (b):

Here $m = 4$, so Borda points are $3, 2, 1, 0$.

**Step 1**: Tally first-place votes for Plurality.

| Candidate | First-Place Votes |
|---|---|
| P | 3 |
| Q | 2 |
| R | 2 |
| S | 2 |

**Plurality winner**: $P$ with 3 first-place votes.

**Step 2**: Compute Borda scores.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}(P) &= (3)(3) + (2)(0) + (2)(2) + (2)(2) = 9 + 0 + 4 + 4 = 17 \\
\text{Score}(Q) &= (3)(2) + (2)(3) + (2)(1) + (2)(2) = 6 + 6 + 2 + 4 = 18 \\
\text{Score}(R) &= (3)(1) + (2)(2) + (2)(3) + (2)(1) = 3 + 4 + 6 + 2 = 15 \\
\text{Score}(S) &= (3)(0) + (2)(1) + (2)(2) + (2)(3) = 0 + 2 + 4 + 6 = 12
\end{aligned}$$

**Borda winner**: $Q$ with 18 points.

**Step 3**: Plurality with Runoff.

- Top 2 by plurality: $P$ (3 votes) and tie among $Q, R, S$ (2 each). Break tie alphabetically — $Q$ advances.
- Pairwise contest $P$ vs $Q$ among all 9 voters:
  - Voters preferring $P$ over $Q$: 3 (Group 1: $P$ top) + 2 (Group 4: $S \succ P \succ Q$, so $P \succ Q$) = 5
  - Voters preferring $Q$ over $P$: 2 (Group 2) + 2 (Group 3: $R \succ S \succ P \succ Q$, so $P \succ Q$) — wait, recheck.

Recheck Group 3: $R \succ S \succ P \succ Q$, so this group prefers $P$ over $Q$.
Recheck Group 4: $S \succ P \succ Q \succ R$, so this group prefers $P$ over $Q$.

So votes for $P \succ Q$: $3 + 2 + 2 = 7$; votes for $Q \succ P$: $2$.

**Runoff winner**: $P$ (7 vs 2).

**Final summary**:

| Rule | Winner |
|---|---|
| Plurality | $P$ |
| Borda | $Q$ |
| Runoff | $P$ |

**Comment (3 marks for full marks)**: Different rules yield different winners even on the same profile — this is **voting paradox / non-agreement**. It demonstrates that the choice of *aggregation rule itself* matters and there is no "neutral" or "objective" aggregation. This motivates the design of strategy-proof mechanisms and impossibility theorems.

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Gibbard–Satterthwaite & Strategy-Proof Mechanisms

**(a)** State and explain the **Gibbard–Satterthwaite Theorem**. Use an illustrative example to show how a non-dictatorial voting rule can be manipulated. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Consider a housing allocation problem with 3 agents and 3 houses. Describe the **Serial Dictatorship (SD)** mechanism and the **Top Trading Cycles (TTC)** mechanism. Given the preferences below, determine the allocation under both mechanisms and verify strategy-proofness. **[7 Marks]**

- Agent 1: $h_3 \succ h_1 \succ h_2$
- Agent 2: $h_1 \succ h_2 \succ h_3$
- Agent 3: $h_2 \succ h_3 \succ h_1$

Priority order $\pi = (1, 2, 3)$ for SD.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Gibbard–Satterthwaite Theorem (3 marks)**: 
*Let $f: \mathcal{L}^n \to A$ be a social choice function defined on the domain of all strict ordinal preferences over a set $A$ of at least three alternatives. If $f$ is (i) **onto** (every alternative can be chosen for some profile) and (ii) **strategy-proof** (truth-telling is a dominant strategy for every agent), then $f$ is **dictatorial** — there exists an agent $d$ such that $f(R)$ is always $d$'s top-ranked alternative.*

**Explanation (2 marks)**: This means any "reasonable" non-dictatorial voting rule with 3+ alternatives must be vulnerable to strategic manipulation. The only way to achieve strategy-proofness is through dictatorship (or trivial/silly rules).

**Illustrative manipulation example (2 marks)**: Under **Plurality** with alternatives $\{A, B, C\}$ and 3 voters:
- True preferences:
  - Voter 1: $A \succ B \succ C$
  - Voter 2: $B \succ A \succ C$
  - Voter 3: $B \succ A \succ C$

Truthful reporting: $A$ gets 1 vote, $B$ gets 2 votes → $B$ wins (Voter 1 loses).

Voter 1 manipulates by reporting $B \succ A \succ C$ instead. Now $B$ gets 1, $A$ gets 1, $B$ gets 1 → Tie. With even slightly more voters in a similar profile, Voter 1's manipulation can shift the result.

A cleaner manipulation: in Borda, a voter can "bury" a strong rival by ranking them last, gaining an advantage.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Serial Dictatorship (SD) mechanism (1.5 marks)**: 
Agents pick houses in a fixed priority order. Agent $\pi(1)$ picks their top choice from all houses; agent $\pi(2)$ picks from the remainder; and so on. The outcome is deterministic and the priority is exogenous.

**Top Trading Cycles (TTC) mechanism (1.5 marks)**: 
Each remaining agent points to the agent occupying their most-preferred remaining house. The mechanism identifies a directed cycle in this "points-to" graph and executes the trade. Repeated until all agents are matched.

**Step-by-step SD allocation (2 marks)**:

- Round 1, Agent 1 picks from $\{h_1, h_2, h_3\}$: top is $h_3$. Agent 1 gets $h_3$.
- Round 2, Agent 2 picks from $\{h_1, h_2\}$: top is $h_1$. Agent 2 gets $h_1$.
- Round 3, Agent 3 gets the last house $h_2$. Agent 3 gets $h_2$.

**SD allocation**: Agent 1 → $h_3$, Agent 2 → $h_1$, Agent 3 → $h_2$.

**Step-by-step TTC allocation (2 marks)**:

- Initial graph: Agent 1 → occupant of $h_3$ (which is currently Agent 3 in some initial endowment; assume endowments $e_1 = h_1, e_2 = h_2, e_3 = h_3$).
  - Agent 1 wants $h_3$ (occupied by Agent 3) → Agent 1 points to Agent 3
  - Agent 2 wants $h_1$ (occupied by Agent 1) → Agent 2 points to Agent 1
  - Agent 3 wants $h_2$ (occupied by Agent 2) → Agent 3 points to Agent 2
- Cycle detected: $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 1$.
- Execute trade: Agent 1 gets $h_3$, Agent 3 gets $h_2$, Agent 2 gets $h_1$.

**TTC allocation**: Agent 1 → $h_3$, Agent 2 → $h_1$, Agent 3 → $h_2$.

**Verification of strategy-proofness (1 mark)**: Both SD and TTC produce the **same allocation here**, and both are **dominant-strategy incentive compatible**. In SD, no agent can gain by misreporting because later agents cannot reverse earlier picks. In TTC, each agent's "top remaining" choice is the truthful best, since pretending to prefer another house does not improve their matched house.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks**:
> 1. **Confusing "ordinal" with "cardinal"** when defining rank-order mechanisms — *always emphasize that NO utility numbers are used*.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify the onto/surjective condition** in Gibbard–Satterthwaite — dictatorship only emerges when $f$ is onto with $\geq 3$ alternatives.
> 3. **Mixing up Arrow's theorem (for SWFs) with GS theorem (for SCFs)** — these are distinct impossibility results.
> 4. **Not showing the full "Step 1, Step 2, ..." structure** in TTC and SD worked examples — examiners award marks for *algorithm clarity*.
> 5. **Claiming Plurality or Borda is strategy-proof** — these are classic non-SP rules and must be cited as such.
> 6. **Forgetting that a Condorcet winner may not exist** — Condorcet's *method* is well-defined, but the *winner* can be absent.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

| # | Concept | Key Take-Home |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rank Ordering | Mechanism where agents submit **ordinal rankings**, not numerical utilities |
| 2 | Ordinal vs Cardinal | Order matters; magnitudes irrelevant in rank-order |
| 3 | SCF $f: \mathcal{L}^n \to A$ | Maps profile of rankings to single alternative |
| 4 | SWF $F: \mathcal{L}^n \to \mathcal{L}$ | Maps profile to full social ranking |
| 5 | Plurality | Top-choice counting; not strategy-proof |
| 6 | Borda Count | $(m - \text{rank})$ sum; not strategy-proof |
| 7 | Veto | Bottom-choice elimination; not strategy-proof |
| 8 | Plurality Runoff | Top-2 + pairwise; not strategy-proof |
| 9 | Condorcet Winner | Beats all pairwise; may not exist (paradox) |
| 10 | Condorcet Paradox | Cyclic majority $A \succ_M B \succ_M C \succ_M A$ |
| 11 | Strategy-Proofness (DSIC) | Truth-telling is dominant strategy |
| 12 | Pareto Efficiency | If all prefer $a$ to $b$, society does too |
| 13 | IIA | Social pairwise ranking depends only on individual pairwise |
| 14 | Non-Dictatorship | No single agent dictates outcome |
| 15 | Arrow's Theorem | Unanimity + IIA + Non-dictatorship + UD ⇒ Impossible |
| 16 | Gibbard–Satterthwaite | Onto + SP + $m \geq 3$ ⇒ Dictatorship |
| 17 | Serial Dictatorship | **SP** priority-based allocation |
| 18 | Top Trading Cycles | **SP** cycle-based housing assignment |
| 19 | Anonymity | Symmetry over agents |
| 20 | Neutrality | Symmetry over alternatives |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Mnemonic — "GAS APPS"** for the four key theorems/conditions of Module 3:
> - **G**ibbard–Satterthwaite (SCF, dictatorship)
> - **A**rrow's (SWF, dictatorship)
> - **S**ingle-Peaked preferences (escape from impossibility)
> - **S**erial Dictatorship / TTC (SP allocation)
> - **P**lurality, **P**areto, **P**airwise (voting-rule vocabulary)

**End of Module 3 — Rank Ordering Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inductive Reasoning, Defuzzification to Scalars - Max Membership Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST753 - GAME THEORY AND MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inductive Reasoning, Defuzzification to Scalars - Max Membership Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Inductive Reasoning in Fuzzy Decision Systems

**Inductive Reasoning** is a logical method in which generalized conclusions are derived from specific observations or evidence. Within the context of fuzzy game theory and mechanism design, inductive reasoning is the cognitive engine that allows an agent (or system) to construct fuzzy rules from observed strategic interactions, payoff patterns, or partial equilibria.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Inductive Reasoning* is the process of moving from a finite set of observed data points or sampled fuzzy membership values to a generalized rule, fuzzy set, or strategic behavior pattern that explains or predicts future observations with an associated confidence (membership grade).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are a chess player who has watched 1,000 games of Magnus Carlsen. From those games, you *induce* a general rule: "When the king is cornered and the queen is mobile, Carlsen sacrifices the queen." You did not derive this from axioms; you *induced* it from patterns. Similarly, in fuzzy systems, we induce fuzzy IF–THEN rules from input-output pairs.

### 1.2 Defuzzification to Scalars

A fuzzy inference system (FIS) produces a **fuzzy set** as its output (e.g., "the temperature is somewhat high"). However, real engineering mechanisms (motors, thermostats, auctions, payment rules) require a **single crisp scalar number** to act upon. The mapping from a fuzzy output set back to a single crisp value is called **Defuzzification**.

$$z^* = f(\tilde{B})$$

where $\tilde{B}$ is the aggregated fuzzy output set and $z^*$ is the defuzzified crisp scalar.

### 1.3 Max Membership Principle

The **Max Membership Principle** (also called the *Height Method*) is the simplest defuzzification technique. It selects the crisp value at which the membership function of the aggregated fuzzy output reaches its **maximum value (peak)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> The Max Membership Principle defuzzifies a fuzzy set $\tilde{B}$ by returning the value $z^*$ in the universe of discourse $Z$ at which $\mu_{\tilde{B}}(z)$ attains its supremum (maximum).

$$z^* = \arg\max_{z \in Z} \; \mu_{\tilde{B}}(z)$$

**Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
Think of a sound-level fuzzy controller that outputs "Volume is Loud" with a triangular membership peaking at 80 dB. When the defuzzifier fires, it simply asks: "At what crisp value is the 'Loud' set strongest?" The answer, 80 dB, is sent to the speaker amplifier. The Max Membership Principle ignores the *shape* of the curve entirely — it only cares about the **peak**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Highlighted:**
> The crisp output $z^*$ lies within the **universe of discourse $Z$** (the range of valid crisp values, e.g., $Z = [0^\circ C, 100^\circ C]$).
> **Computational Cost:** $O(n)$ — the cheapest of all defuzzification methods.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Inductive Reasoning — Operational Logic

Inductive reasoning, when applied to fuzzy rule induction, follows a structured pipeline:

- **Step 1 — Observation Collection:** Gather input-output pairs $(x_i, y_i)$ from historical data or game-play logs.
- **Step 2 — Fuzzification:** Convert each crisp $x_i, y_i$ into fuzzy membership vectors.
- **Step 3 — Rule Induction:** Use algorithms (Wang–Mendel, ANFIS, or genetic fuzzy systems) to induce IF–THEN rules.
- **Step 4 — Rule Aggregation:** Combine induced rules into a single fuzzy output set $\tilde{B}$.
- **Step 5 — Defuzzification:** Convert $\tilde{B}$ to a crisp scalar $z^*$ that drives the mechanism.

**Why Inductive Reasoning Fails in Game Theory (Critical Caveat):**
In strategic games, opponents *anticipate* your induced pattern. If your rule says "I always bid 80% of my valuation," a rational opponent exploits it. This is why inductive reasoning must be coupled with **mechanism design constraints** (incentive compatibility) — a core theme of PECST753.

### 2.2 Max Membership Principle — Why and How

**The "Why":**
It is computationally trivial and intuitive. When the fuzzy output has a clear single peak (unimodal, normal fuzzy set), the peak represents the *most representative* crisp value.

**The "How":**
- Compute the aggregated membership function $\mu_{\tilde{B}}(z)$ over $Z$.
- Scan or analytically solve for the point(s) where $\mu_{\tilde{B}}(z)$ is maximum.
- If multiple peaks exist with the same maximum value, take the **mean of all peaks** (this modified version is called the *Mean of Maxima*, discussed in adjacent topics).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **When Used** | **Limitation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inductive Generalization | $P(h \mid D) \propto P(D \mid h) P(h)$ | Bayesian rule induction from data | Requires prior $P(h)$ |
| Defuzzification (Generic) | $z^* = f(\mu_{\tilde{B}}(z))$ | Any fuzzy output conversion | Method-dependent bias |
| Max Membership Principle | $z^* = \arg\max_{z} \mu_{\tilde{B}}(z)$ | Unimodal, sharply peaked sets | Ignores set *shape* and *support* |
| Mean of Maxima (variant) | $z^* = \frac{1}{\vert M \vert}\sum_{z \in M} z$, where $M = \{z : \mu_{\tilde{B}}(z) = h\}$ | Multimodal sets with equal peaks | Loses peak-height contrast |
| Centroid (contrast) | $z^* = \frac{\int z \cdot \mu_{\tilde{B}}(z)\, dz}{\int \mu_{\tilde{B}}(z)\, dz}$ | Smooth, continuous sets | Higher computation $O(n \log n)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> Max Membership is used in real-time **PID-fuzzy motor controllers**, **automotive anti-lock braking fuzzy modules**, and **auction-bid fuzzy triggers** where latency is critical and the output fuzzy set is engineered to have a single dominant peak.

### 2.4 Tie-Breaking Rule (Subtlety Examiners Love)

When $\mu_{\tilde{B}}(z)$ attains its supremum at multiple isolated points $\{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_k\}$:

$$z^* = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} z_i$$

This is the *Mean of Maxima* fallback and frequently appears in KTU numerical problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> The KTU 2024 PECST753 syllabus lists Max Membership Principle explicitly as the entry-point defuzzification technique. Students are expected to compute $z^*$ analytically for triangular and trapezoidal fuzzy sets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation — Triangular Fuzzy Set

Consider a triangular fuzzy set $\tilde{B}$ on universe $Z = [0, 10]$ defined as:

$$
\mu_{\tilde{B}}(z) =
\begin{cases}
0, & z < 4 \\
\dfrac{z - 4}{6 - 4} = \dfrac{z - 4}{2}, & 4 \le z \le 6 \\
\dfrac{8 - z}{8 - 6} = \dfrac{8 - z}{2}, & 6 \le z \le 8 \\
0, & z > 8
\end{cases}
$$

This triangle peaks at $z = 6$ with $\mu_{\tilde{B}}(6) = 1$.

**Step 1 — Identify the domain pieces.**

The piecewise function has three linear pieces on $[4,6]$, $[6,8]$, and zero elsewhere.

**Step 2 — Find the supremum of $\mu_{\tilde{B}}(z)$.**

On $[4,6]$, the function increases linearly from $0$ to $1$, so its maximum on this piece is at $z=6$.

On $[6,8]$, the function decreases linearly from $1$ to $0$, so its maximum on this piece is also at $z=6$.

**Step 3 — Take the global supremum.**

$$\max_{z \in Z} \mu_{\tilde{B}}(z) = 1 \quad \text{attained at } z = 6$$

**Step 4 — Apply Max Membership Principle.**

$$z^* = \arg\max_{z \in Z} \mu_{\tilde{B}}(z) = 6$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Trapezoidal Fuzzy Set (Multimodal Case)

Let $\tilde{B}$ on $Z = [0, 20]$ be a flat-top trapezoid:

$$
\mu_{\tilde{B}}(z) =
\begin{cases}
0, & z < 2 \\
z - 2, & 2 \le z \le 3 \\
1, & 3 \le z \le 7 \\
12 - z, & 7 \le z \le 12 \\
0, & z > 12
\end{cases}
$$

The supremum value is $1$, attained on the entire flat-top interval $[3, 7]$.

**Step 1 — Identify the plateau.**

$$M = \{z \in Z : \mu_{\tilde{B}}(z) = 1\} = [3, 7]$$

This is an infinite (uncountable) set of maximizers.

**Step 2 — Apply Mean of Maxima fallback.**

$$z^* = \frac{\int_3^7 z \, dz}{7 - 3} = \frac{\left[\frac{z^2}{2}\right]_3^7}{4} = \frac{\frac{49}{2} - \frac{9}{2}}{4} = \frac{20}{4} = 5$$

$$\boxed{z^* = 5}$$

**Step 3 — Verification.**

By symmetry, the trapezoid is symmetric about $z = 5$, so $z^* = 5$ is intuitively correct. ✓

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Defuzzification Method Selection

| **Property** | **Max Membership** | **Centroid** | **Weighted Average** | **Mean of Maxima** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Computational Cost | **Lowest** $O(n)$ | Highest $O(n \log n)$ | Low $O(n)$ | Low $O(n)$ |
| Sensitivity to Shape | None | High | Medium | None (uses only peaks) |
| Handles Multimodal | Poor | Excellent | Medium | Good (averages peaks) |
| Output Stability | Moderate | High | High | Moderate |
| Best Use Case | Single-peak sets | Continuous output | Few singletons | Multi-peak sets |

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference for Mechanism Design:**
> In auction mechanisms where the fuzzy output represents *bidder enthusiasm*, a unimodal enthusiasm curve (single dominant peak) justifies Max Membership. If bidders split into two distinct clusters (multimodal), the Mean of Maxima or Centroid is preferred.

### 3.4 Linking Inductive Reasoning to Defuzzification

The logical chain in an adaptive fuzzy game-theoretic agent is:

$$
\text{Observed Plays} \xrightarrow{\text{Induction}} \text{Fuzzy Rule Base} \xrightarrow{\text{Inference}} \tilde{B} \xrightarrow{\text{Defuzzification}} z^* \xrightarrow{\text{Mechanism}} \text{Action}
$$

The Max Membership step is the final bridge from fuzzy cognition to crisp mechanism execution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Inductive Fuzzy Defuzzification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed Strategic Data] --> B[Fuzzification Layer]
    B --> C[Fuzzy Rule Induction via Inductive Reasoning]
    C --> D[Aggregated Fuzzy Output Set B-tilde]
    D --> E{Output Shape Analysis}
    E -->|Unimodal Peak| F[Max Membership Principle]
    E -->|Multimodal Peaks| G[Mean of Maxima Fallback]
    E -->|Smooth Continuous| H[Centroid Method]
    F --> I[Crisp Scalar z-star]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Mechanism Action - Bid, Payoff, Strategy]
    J --> K[Feedback to Inductive Learner]
    K --> A
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic for Defuzzification Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuzzy Set B-tilde] --> B{Is mu-B-tilde unimodal?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Is peak sharp?}
    B -->|No| D{Multiple equal peaks?}
    C -->|Yes| E[Apply Max Membership]
    C -->|No| F[Apply Centroid]
    D -->|Yes| G[Apply Mean of Maxima]
    D -->|No| H[Apply Centroid with weighted peaks]
    E --> Z[Output z-star]
    F --> Z
    G --> Z
    H --> Z
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Max Membership Internals

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input
        direction TB
        node1[Fuzzy Set B-tilde as mu-of-z]
    end
    subgraph Processing
        direction TB
        node2[Compute h equals sup of mu-B-tilde]
        node3[Find set M of all z where mu equals h]
        node4{Is M a singleton?}
        node5[Return z-star equals unique element]
        node6[Apply Mean of Maxima over M]
    end
    subgraph Output
        direction TB
        node7[Crisp Scalar z-star]
    end
    node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4
    node4 -->|Yes| node5 --> node7
    node4 -->|No| node6 --> node7
```

### 4.4 Inductive Reasoning Loop in Mechanism Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Past Game Outcomes] --> P2[Extract Fuzzy Patterns]
    P2 --> P3[Induce IF-THEN Fuzzy Rules]
    P3 --> P4[Store in Rule Base]
    P4 --> P5[New Game Scenario Encountered]
    P5 --> P6[Fuzzy Inference Engine Activated]
    P6 --> P7[Aggregated Fuzzy Output]
    P7 --> P8[Max Membership Defuzzification]
    P8 --> P9[Crisp Strategic Decision]
    P9 --> P10[Execute Mechanism]
    P10 --> P11[Observe New Outcome]
    P11 --> P1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define *Inductive Reasoning* in the context of fuzzy game-theoretic systems.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Inductive Reasoning is a logical process of deriving general fuzzy rules, patterns, or strategic behaviors from a finite set of specific observations or sampled data. In PECST753, it is used by an agent to construct a fuzzy rule base from historical game outcomes, enabling the agent to predict opponent moves and select optimal actions via fuzzy inference followed by defuzzification. *(1 mark definition, 1 mark context, 1 mark example/linkage)*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** State the *Max Membership Principle* of defuzzification. Mention one limitation.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Max Membership Principle defuzzifies a fuzzy set $\tilde{B}$ by returning the crisp value $z^*$ at which the membership function $\mu_{\tilde{B}}(z)$ attains its maximum (supremum). Mathematically, $z^* = \arg\max_{z \in Z} \mu_{\tilde{B}}(z)$. *(2 marks)*
> **Limitation:** It ignores the shape and support of the fuzzy set, considering only the peak location; thus it is unsuitable for multimodal fuzzy outputs. *(1 mark)*

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]

**(a)** Explain the role of *Inductive Reasoning* in building a fuzzy rule base for a strategic decision-making agent. Discuss its connection to mechanism design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuzzy output set $\tilde{B}$ on universe $Z = [0, 10]$ is described by:
$$
\mu_{\tilde{B}}(z) =
\begin{cases}
0, & z < 1 \\
2z - 2, & 1 \le z \le 1.5 \\
1, & 1.5 \le z \le 4.5 \\
9 - z, & 4.5 \le z \le 9 \\
0, & z > 9
\end{cases}
$$
Apply the **Max Membership Principle** to compute the crisp output $z^*$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Solution (7 marks):**
- Inductive Reasoning enables the agent to extract fuzzy IF–THEN rules from historical game data, building a rule base $R = \{R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_m\}$. *(2 marks)*
- Each rule has the form: IF (state is $A_i$) THEN (action is $B_i$), where $A_i, B_i$ are fuzzy sets. *(1 mark)*
- In mechanism design, the agent uses these induced rules to map observed game states into strategic actions, while the mechanism designer ensures the rules align with **incentive-compatible** outcomes (truthfulness). *(2 marks)*
- The induced rule base is continuously refined via feedback from new game outcomes, closing the inductive loop. *(2 marks)*

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the supremum value of $\mu_{\tilde{B}}(z)$.**

On the rising piece $[1, 1.5]$, $\mu$ increases from $0$ to $1$.
On the flat-top piece $[1.5, 4.5]$, $\mu = 1$ constantly.
On the falling piece $[4.5, 9]$, $\mu$ decreases from $1$ to $0$.

Therefore:
$$\sup_{z \in Z} \mu_{\tilde{B}}(z) = 1$$

**Step 2 — Identify the maximizer set $M$.**

$$M = \{z \in Z : \mu_{\tilde{B}}(z) = 1\} = [1.5, 4.5]$$

**Step 3 — Since $M$ is not a singleton, apply the Mean of Maxima fallback.**

$$z^* = \frac{\int_{1.5}^{4.5} z \, dz}{4.5 - 1.5} = \frac{\left[\frac{z^2}{2}\right]_{1.5}^{4.5}}{3}$$

Compute the numerator:
$$\frac{(4.5)^2 - (1.5)^2}{2} = \frac{20.25 - 2.25}{2} = \frac{18}{2} = 9$$

Therefore:
$$z^* = \frac{9}{3} = 3$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer.**

$$\boxed{z^* = 3}$$

By symmetry of the trapezoid about $z = 3$, this result is consistent. *(Verification: 1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**(a)** Differentiate between *Inductive Reasoning* and *Deductive Reasoning* in fuzzy systems. Why is inductive reasoning preferred for adaptive agents? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The aggregated fuzzy output of a temperature controller is:
$$
\mu_{\tilde{B}}(z) =
\begin{cases}
0, & z < 20 \\
\dfrac{z - 20}{10}, & 20 \le z \le 30 \\
\dfrac{40 - z}{10}, & 30 \le z \le 40 \\
0, & z > 40
\end{cases}
$$
Use the **Max Membership Principle** to obtain the defuzzified temperature setting. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Solution (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Inductive Reasoning | Deductive Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Specific → General | General → Specific |
| Certainty | Probabilistic / Fuzzy | Logical / Crisp |
| Data Need | Requires historical observations | Requires axioms / premises |
| Use in Fuzzy Systems | Rule induction from data | Rule application to new inputs |

- Inductive Reasoning moves from observed data points to generalized fuzzy rules; deductive reasoning applies known rules to derive specific outputs. *(2 marks)*
- Inductive reasoning handles **uncertainty** via fuzzy membership grades, while deductive reasoning in pure form cannot. *(2 marks)*
- Adaptive agents in dynamic games face *unknown opponent strategies*; only induction can discover hidden patterns, making it indispensable. *(2 marks)*
- Deductive reasoning is used *after* induction — once rules exist, deduction applies them. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the triangular shape.**

The fuzzy set is a triangle with base $[20, 40]$ and peak at $z = 30$ (since $\mu(30) = \frac{30-20}{10} = 1$ and also $\mu(30) = \frac{40-30}{10} = 1$).

**Step 2 — Find the supremum.**

$$\max_{z \in Z} \mu_{\tilde{B}}(z) = 1 \quad \text{attained at } z = 30$$

**Step 3 — Apply Max Membership Principle.**

$$z^* = \arg\max_{z \in Z} \mu_{\tilde{B}}(z) = 30$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer.**

$$\boxed{z^* = 30^\circ C}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Mean-of-Maxima fallback (–2 marks):** When the fuzzy set has a flat top or multiple peaks, students often pick one endpoint arbitrarily. Always check if the maximizer set is a singleton.
> 2. **Confusing Max Membership with Centroid (–3 marks):** These are *different* methods. Max Membership uses only the peak; Centroid uses the entire area. Do not mix them.
> 3. **Omitting the integration step (–1 mark):** In flat-top trapezoidal cases, explicit integration of $\int_M z\, dz$ is required and must be shown.
> 4. **Ignoring piecewise boundaries (–1 mark):** Always verify the piecewise domain boundaries before claiming a peak.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inductive Reasoning** = data-driven rule discovery (specific → general), central to adaptive fuzzy agents.
- **Defuzzification** = conversion of fuzzy output set $\tilde{B}$ into a crisp scalar $z^*$.
- **Max Membership Principle** = $z^* = \arg\max_z \mu_{\tilde{B}}(z)$.
- **When to use Max Membership:** unimodal, sharply peaked fuzzy sets; latency-critical applications.
- **When NOT to use:** multimodal sets (use Mean of Maxima) or smooth continuous sets (use Centroid).
- **Mean of Maxima fallback:** $z^* = \frac{1}{\vert M \vert}\sum_{z \in M} z$ when multiple peaks tie.
- **Computational cost** of Max Membership is $O(n)$ — the lowest among defuzzification methods.
- **Limitation:** Discards the shape and area of the fuzzy set, retaining only peak location.
- **Engineering linkage:** Real-time fuzzy controllers (motors, ABS, HVAC) and auction bidding mechanisms.
- **In mechanism design:** Inductive rule bases must satisfy **incentive compatibility** — otherwise opponents exploit the discovered pattern.
- **Examiner hot tip:** Always verify if the maximizer set $M$ is a singleton before concluding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centroid Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST753 - GAME THEORY AND MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Centroid Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Centroid Method)**
> The **Centroid Method** is an iterative algorithmic procedure in algorithmic game theory and online learning in which, at every time step $t$, a player plays the **centroid** (i.e., the uniform arithmetic mean) of the entire history of mixed strategies that she has played up to step $t$. Formally, if the player's sequence of mixed strategies is $\sigma^{(1)}, \sigma^{(2)}, \dots, \sigma^{(T)}$, then the strategy played at the end of horizon $T$ is reported as the **time-averaged strategy** $\bar{\sigma}^{(T)} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\sigma^{(t)}$.

This method is intimately tied to two foundational pillars:
1. **Hannan Consistency** — A learning rule is *Hannan consistent* if its time-averaged regret converges in probability to zero against every fixed opponent strategy.
2. **Approximate Nash Equilibrium Computation** — In two-player zero-sum games, the time-averaged play under the Centroid Method provably converges to a Nash equilibrium of the game.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Experienced Trader"**
> Imagine a stock trader who has tried **100 different portfolios** over the past year. Some were brilliant, some were terrible. Instead of stubbornly sticking to the very last (often lucky or unlucky) portfolio, she reports the **average of all 100 portfolios** as her "wisdom strategy." This average is her *centroid* — a balanced, diversified, hindsight-smoothed decision that is statistically more robust than any single past choice. Similarly, in a repeated game, the Centroid Method smooths out the noise of individual rounds by reporting the average strategy.

**Standard Metric Highlighted:**
- The key performance metric is the **average per-round regret**, defined as $R_T = \max_{x \in \Delta} \sum_{t=1}^{T} u(x, y^{(t)}) - \sum_{t=1}^{T} u(x^{(t)}, y^{(t)})$.
- The Centroid Method achieves an average regret of $O(1/\sqrt{T})$, vanishing as $T \to \infty$.

## 1.3 Why Centroid Method? — Syllabus Significance

In KTU PECST753 Module 3, the Centroid Method sits at the crossroads of **mechanism design** (designing rules so that honest behavior is optimal) and **algorithmic game theory** (computing equilibria). It demonstrates that simple, decentralized learning can yield globally optimal (Nash) outcomes — a cornerstone of modern mechanism design known as the **surrogate for equilibrium computation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Structured Logic Steps

The Centroid Method operates on the following canonical logic pipeline:

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** At time $t = 1$, the player selects an arbitrary initial mixed strategy $\sigma^{(1)} \in \Delta(S)$, where $\Delta(S)$ is the probability simplex over the action set $S$.
- **Step 2 — Observation:** At each round $t$, the player observes the opponent's action (or strategy) and computes her realized payoff $u(\sigma^{(t)}, y^{(t)})$.
- **Step 3 — Counterfactual Update:** The player computes, for every pure action $a \in S$, the cumulative regret:
$$R_T(a) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \big[ u(a, y^{(t)}) - u(\sigma^{(t)}, y^{(t)}) \big]$$
- **Step 4 — Next-Step Strategy:** The next mixed strategy $\sigma^{(t+1)}$ is selected via a no-regret learning rule (often regret matching or multiplicative weights).
- **Step 5 — Centroid Reporting:** At horizon $T$, the algorithm **reports** the time-averaged strategy $\bar{\sigma}^{(T)} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\sigma^{(t)}$, which is the *centroid*.
- **Step 6 — Equilibrium Claim:** For two-player zero-sum games, the sequence $\{\bar{\sigma}^{(T)}\}_{T=1}^{\infty}$ converges to a Nash equilibrium.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why initialize arbitrarily?** Because any initial strategy is asymptotically irrelevant — the centroid averages it out.
- **Why track cumulative regret?** Because regret is the gap between what was earned and what could have been earned by always playing a single best fixed action in hindsight.
- **Why report the average?** By the **Strong Law of Large Numbers (SLLN)** and Blackwell's approachability theorem, the average strategy inherits convergence guarantees that individual iterates lack.
- **Why does this give a Nash equilibrium?** In zero-sum games, the **Minimax Theorem** guarantees $\min_{\sigma}\max_{\tau} u(\sigma,\tau) = \max_{\tau}\min_{\sigma} u(\sigma,\tau) = v$, and the centroid, being a no-regret strategy, asymptotically achieves this value from both sides.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table**

| # | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Boundary / Domain |
|---|------------------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\Delta(S) = \{ \sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert S \vert} : \sigma \geq 0,\ \sum_a \sigma_a = 1\}$ | Mixed strategy simplex | $a \in S$, $\sigma_a \geq 0$ |
| 2 | $\bar{\sigma}^{(T)} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} \sigma^{(t)}$ | Centroid (time-averaged strategy) | $T \geq 1$ |
| 3 | $R_T(a) = \sum_{t=1}^{T}[ u(a, y^{(t)}) - u(\sigma^{(t)}, y^{(t)}) ]$ | Cumulative regret of action $a$ | $a \in S$ |
| 4 | $R_T^{+} = \max_{a \in S} R_T(a)$ | Maximum cumulative regret | $\geq 0$ |
| 5 | $\overline{R}_T = R_T^{+} / T$ | Average regret (per round) | $\to 0$ as $T \to \infty$ |
| 6 | $u(\bar{\sigma}^{(T)}, \bar{\tau}^{(T)}) \to v$ | Exploitability of centroid (zero-sum) | $v$ = game value |
| 7 | $\overline{R}_T \leq O(1/\sqrt{T})$ | Regret bound (Hannan consistency) | Standard rate |
| 8 | $u(a, y)$ | Payoff to player from action $a$ vs $y$ | Bounded in $[0,1]$ (WLOG) |
| 9 | $\sigma^{(t+1)}_a \propto \max\{0, R_t(a)\}$ | Regret-Matching next strategy | Un-normalized |
| 10 | $\lim_{T\to\infty} \bar{\sigma}^{(T)} = \sigma^{\ast}$ | Centroid convergence to Nash $\sigma^{\ast}$ | Zero-sum only |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Centroid Method is not just a theoretical curiosity — it is the algorithmic engine behind:

- **Online Ad Auctions (Google, Meta):** Online bidders use centroid-style averaging to approximate equilibrium bidding strategies in repeated second-price and first-price auctions.
- **Congestion Routing in Networks:** TCP congestion control can be modeled as no-regret learners whose centroid behavior converges to a Wardrop equilibrium.
- **Mechanism Design for Crowdsourcing:** Platforms like Amazon Mechanical Turk use centroid-of-bids logic to robustly aggregate noisy worker responses.
- **Algorithmic Pricing in E-commerce:** Dynamic pricing algorithms (e.g., for airline tickets) deploy no-regret updates; their time-averaged policy converges to the monopoly/mechanism-design optimal price.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Centroid's Equilibrium Guarantee (Two-Player Zero-Sum Game)

**Setup.** Let $G = (A, B, u)$ be a finite two-player zero-sum game. Player 1 (row) plays $x \in \Delta(A)$, Player 2 (column) plays $y \in \Delta(B)$. Payoffs are in $[0,1]$. Both players follow a no-regret learning algorithm that produces sequences $\{x^{(t)}\}$ and $\{y^{(t)}\}$.

**Goal.** Prove that the centroid pair $(\bar{x}^{(T)}, \bar{y}^{(T)})$ is an $\varepsilon$-Nash equilibrium with $\varepsilon = O(1/\sqrt{T})$.

---

### Step 1 — Express Best-Response Gap via Regret

For Player 1, the exploitability of $\bar{x}^{(T)}$ against the best column response is:

$$\max_{y \in \Delta(B)} u(\bar{x}^{(T)}, y) - \min_{x \in \Delta(A)} u(x, \bar{y}^{(T)})$$

By the zero-sum property, this equals $\max_{y} u(\bar{x}^{(T)}, y) + \max_{x} u(x, \bar{y}^{(T)})$ (since $-u$ is the column player's payoff).

---

### Step 2 — Swap Max with Average Using Convexity

Because $u$ is bilinear in mixed strategies, for any sequence $\{y^{(t)}\}$:

$$\max_{y \in \Delta(B)} u(\bar{x}^{(T)}, y) \leq \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \max_{y} u(x^{(t)}, y) + \overline{R}_T^{(1)}$$

where $\overline{R}_T^{(1)}$ is Player 1's average regret.

**Reasoning:** The LHS picks the best $y$ against the *average* $\bar{x}$. The RHS averages the best responses to each $x^{(t)}$. The gap is bounded by the regret of playing the fixed best-in-hindsight action.

---

### Step 3 — Use Bilinearity of $u$

$$u(\bar{x}^{(T)}, y) = u\left( \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} x^{(t)},\ y \right) = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} u(x^{(t)}, y)$$

This identity is exact (no inequality needed) by linearity of expectation in the row player's strategy.

---

### Step 4 — Bound the Average Regret

Standard no-regret algorithms (Multiplicative Weights, Hedge, Regret Matching) guarantee:

$$\overline{R}_T^{(1)} \leq \frac{\sqrt{\ln \vert A \vert}}{\sqrt{T}}, \qquad \overline{R}_T^{(2)} \leq \frac{\sqrt{\ln \vert B \vert}}{\sqrt{T}}$$

---

### Step 5 — Combine Into Exploitability Bound

$$\text{Exploitability}(\bar{x}^{(T)}, \bar{y}^{(T)}) \leq \overline{R}_T^{(1)} + \overline{R}_T^{(2)} \leq \frac{\sqrt{\ln \vert A \vert} + \sqrt{\ln \vert B \vert}}{\sqrt{T}}$$

**Interpretation:** To achieve exploitability $\varepsilon$, the algorithm needs only $T = O((\ln \vert A \vert + \ln \vert B \vert) / \varepsilon^2)$ iterations.

---

### Step 6 — Convergence to Nash

Taking the limit $T \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \text{Exploitability}(\bar{x}^{(T)}, \bar{y}^{(T)}) = 0$$

By definition, this means $(\bar{x}^{(T)}, \bar{y}^{(T)}) \to (x^{\ast}, y^{\ast})$ where $(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast})$ is a **Nash equilibrium** of $G$.

$$\boxed{\ (\bar{x}^{(T)}, \bar{y}^{(T)}) \ \xrightarrow{T \to \infty} \ (x^{\ast}, y^{\ast})\ }$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Rock-Paper-Scissors (RPS)

**Game Matrix** (row player payoff, where R=0, P=1, S=2 in cyclic order; "win=+1, lose=−1, draw=0"):

$$\begin{aligned}
u(R, R) &= 0, \quad u(R, P) = -1, \quad u(R, S) = +1 \\
u(P, R) &= +1, \quad u(P, P) = 0, \quad u(P, S) = -1 \\
u(S, R) &= -1, \quad u(S, P) = +1, \quad u(S, S) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Nash Equilibrium (uniform):** $x^{\ast} = y^{\ast} = (1/3, 1/3, 1/3)$, value $v = 0$.

**Centroid Computation — Suppose** Player 1 plays the sequence (deterministic history) $\sigma^{(1)} = R,\ \sigma^{(2)} = P,\ \sigma^{(3)} = S$ (cycling).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{\sigma}^{(3)} &= \frac{1}{3}(R + P + S) \\
&= \frac{1}{3}(1,0,0) + \frac{1}{3}(0,1,0) + \frac{1}{3}(0,0,1) \\
&= \left(\frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{3}\right) \\
&= x^{\ast} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Remark:** Even though every single $\sigma^{(t)}$ is pure and exploitable, the **centroid** is the unique Nash equilibrium. This is the central insight of the method.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Properties Summary

| Property | Statement | KTU Significance |
|----------|-----------|------------------|
| Existence | Centroid $\bar{\sigma}^{(T)}$ always exists | Guaranteed by averaging |
| Boundedness | $\bar{\sigma}^{(T)} \in \Delta(S)$ always | Simplex is closed under averaging |
| Convergence | $\bar{\sigma}^{(T)} \to \sigma^{\ast}$ in zero-sum games | Tight link to Minimax |
| Regret | $\overline{R}_T \leq O(1/\sqrt{T})$ | Hannan consistency |
| Time-Invariant | Only depends on history, not step | Stable report |
| Mechanism Role | Surrogate for incentive-compatible truthful reporting | Used in MACS, Groves |

---
