## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When Each Solution Concept is Appropriate

> [!TIP]
> **Decision Guide for KTU Exam Questions**

| Scenario | Best Tool | Why? |
|----------|-----------|------|
| Centralized planner divides surplus | Shapley Value | Axiomatic, fair, treats symmetric players equally |
| Group wants to minimize max-complaint | Nucleolus | Lexicographically minimizes excesses |
| Decentralized bargaining with first-mover | Larsen Model (SPE) | Captures strategic commitment |
| Two players negotiating under risk | Nash Bargaining Solution | Axiomatic, 2-player specific |
| Alternating-offer infinite horizon | Rubinstein SPE | Discounting creates equilibrium |
| Mechanism designer wants truthfulness | VCG / Myerson | Incentive compatibility, revenue equivalence |
| Coalitions may not form grand coalition | Stable Set / Kernel | Allows non-grand-coalition outcomes |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define the Larsen Model
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Larsen Model** is a non-cooperative game-theoretic framework for analyzing **coalition formation and bargaining** among rational players. It specifies:
> 1. A finite player set $N$ and a **characteristic function** $v: 2^N \to \mathbb{R}$.
> 2. An **extensive-form offer-and-acceptance game** where one player proposes a coalition $S$ and a payoff vector $\mathbf{x}_S$.
> 3. The **Subgame Perfect Equilibrium (SPE)** is computed via backward induction.
> 4. The resulting **Larsen-stable outcome** is the SPE prediction that survives all profitable coalitional deviations.
>
> Unlike the Core (which may be empty) or the Shapley Value (axiomatic, single-point), the Larsen Model produces a **strategically credible** prediction of *which coalitions form* and *how payoffs are divided*, capturing the **first-mover advantage** in real-world negotiations.

### Question A2: Distinguish between the Larsen Model and the Core
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Larsen Model | The Core |
|---------|--------------|----------|
| Solution concept | **SPE (non-cooperative)** | **Coalitional rationality (axiomatic)** |
| Existence | **Always exists** (by Zermelo's theorem) | **May be empty** |
| Granularity | Predicts **coalition structure + payoff vector** | Predicts only **set of imputations** |
| Computational method | **Backward induction** | Linear feasibility + linear programming |
| Captures first-mover advantage | **Yes** | No |
| Captures strategic commitment | **Yes** | No |
| Best suited for | **Decentralized bargaining settings** | Centralized cooperative planning |

> The **Core is stronger** (more demanding), but the **Larsen Model is more general** (always applicable) and **more realistic** in non-cooperative bargaining.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Larsen Model Analysis for 4-Player Game**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

A TU-game has 4 players with the following characteristic function values:
$$v(\{1\}) = 5, \ v(\{2\}) = 4, \ v(\{3\}) = 3, \ v(\{4\}) = 2$$
$$v(\{1,2\}) = 11, \ v(\{1,3\}) = 10, \ v(\{1,4\}) = 8$$
$$v(\{2,3\}) = 9, \ v(\{2,4\}) = 7, \ v(\{3,4\}) = 6$$
$$v(\{1,2,3\}) = 16, \ v(\{1,2,4\}) = 14, \ v(\{1,3,4\}) = 13, \ v(\{2,3,4\}) = 12$$
$$v(\{1,2,3,4\}) = 20$$

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Check whether the game is superadditive. Determine if the grand coalition is always beneficial. Comment on the implication for the Larsen Model.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Suppose Player 1 is the designated proposer in the Larsen extensive-form game. Compute the SPE offer (Player 1's optimal proposal to all other players). Verify Larsen stability of the resulting outcome.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Verify superadditivity for all disjoint $S, T \subseteq N$, $v(S \cup T) \geq v(S) + v(T)$.

For 4 players, there are many such checks. We highlight the critical ones:

- $v(\{1,2\}) = 11 \geq 5 + 4 = 9$ ✅
- $v(\{1,3\}) = 10 \geq 5 + 3 = 8$ ✅
- $v(\{1,4\}) = 8 \geq 5 + 2 = 7$ ✅
- $v(\{2,3\}) = 9 \geq 4 + 3 = 7$ ✅
- $v(\{2,4\}) = 7 \geq 4 + 2 = 6$ ✅
- $v(\{3,4\}) = 6 \geq 3 + 2 = 5$ ✅
- $v(\{1,2,3\}) = 16 \geq v(\{1,2\}) + v(\{3\}) = 11 + 3 = 14$ ✅
- $v(\{1,2,3\}) = 16 \geq v(\{1\}) + v(\{2,3\}) = 5 + 9 = 14$ ✅
- $v(\{1,2,4\}) = 14 \geq v(\{1,2\}) + v(\{4\}) = 11 + 2 = 13$ ✅
- $v(\{1,2,4\}) = 14 \geq v(\{1,4\}) + v(\{2\}) = 8 + 4 = 12$ ✅
- $v(\{1,2,4\}) = 14 \geq v(\{2,4\}) + v(\{1\}) = 7 + 5 = 12$ ✅
- $v(\{1,3,4\}) = 13 \geq v(\{1,3\}) + v(\{4\}) = 10 + 2 = 12$ ✅
- $v(\{1,3,4\}) = 13 \geq v(\{1,4\}) + v(\{3\}) = 8 + 3 = 11$ ✅
- $v(\{1,3,4\}) = 13 \geq v(\{3,4\}) + v(\{1\}) = 6 + 5 = 11$ ✅
- $v(\{2,3,4\}) = 12 \geq v(\{2,3\}) + v(\{4\}) = 9 + 2 = 11$ ✅
- $v(\{2,3,4\}) = 12 \geq v(\{2,4\}) + v(\{3\}) = 7 + 3 = 10$ ✅
- $v(\{2,3,4\}) = 12 \geq v(\{3,4\}) + v(\{2\}) = 6 + 4 = 10$ ✅
- $v(\{1,2,3,4\}) = 20 \geq v(\{1,2,3\}) + v(\{4\}) = 16 + 2 = 18$ ✅
- $v(\{1,2,3,4\}) = 20 \geq v(\{1,2,4\}) + v(\{3\}) = 14 + 3 = 17$ ✅
- $v(\{1,2,3,4\}) = 20 \geq v(\{1,3,4\}) + v(\{2\}) = 13 + 4 = 17$ ✅
- $v(\{1,2,3,4\}) = 20 \geq v(\{2,3,4\}) + v(\{1\}) = 12 + 5 = 17$ ✅
- $v(\{1,2,3,4\}) = 20 \geq v(\{1,2\}) + v(\{3,4\}) = 11 + 6 = 17$ ✅
- $v(\{1,2,3,4\}) = 20 \geq v(\{1,3\}) + v(\{2,4\}) = 10 + 7 = 17$ ✅
- $v(\{1,2,3,4\}) = 20 \geq v(\{1,4\}) + v(\{2,3\}) = 8 + 9 = 17$ ✅
- $v(\{1,2,3,4\}) = 20 \geq v(\{1\}) + v(\{2\}) + v(\{3\}) + v(\{4\}) = 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 = 14$ ✅

**Step 2:** The game is **strictly superadditive** (in fact, all inequalities are strict).

**Implication for the Larsen Model:**

> Since the game is superadditive, the **grand coalition is always Pareto-efficient** in terms of total surplus. The Larsen Model will, in equilibrium, predict the formation of the **grand coalition** $N = \{1,2,3,4\}$ with total payoff $v(N) = 20$. The only question is **how this 20 is divided**, which depends on bargaining power and SPE dynamics.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Set up Player 1 as the proposer in the Larsen extensive-form game. The game tree:

- **Stage 1:** Player 1 proposes an imputation $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4)$ for the grand coalition with $x_1 + x_2 + x_3 + x_4 = 20$.
- **Stage 2:** Players 2, 3, 4 each simultaneously accept or reject.
- **Fallback:** If rejected, each gets $v(\{i\})$.

**Step 2:** By backward induction, Players 2, 3, 4 will accept if and only if $x_i \geq v(\{i\})$. Player 1 anticipates this and offers the **minimum** that makes each indifferent.

- $x_2 = v(\{2\}) = 4$
- $x_3 = v(\{3\}) = 3$
- $x_4 = v(\{4\}) = 2$
- $x_1 = 20 - 4 - 3 - 2 = 11$

**SPE Proposal:** $\mathbf{x}^* = (11, 4, 3, 2)$.

**Step 3: Verify Larsen Stability** — check that no coalition $S$ can profitably deviate.

- **Singleton deviations:**
  - Player 1: solo payoff = 5, current = 11. No deviation. ✅
  - Player 2: solo = 4, current = 4. Indifferent. ✅
  - Player 3: solo = 3, current = 3. Indifferent. ✅
  - Player 4: solo = 2, current = 2. Indifferent. ✅

- **Pairwise deviations:**
  - $\{1,2\}$: combined $= 15 > 11$. No deviation. ✅
  - $\{1,3\}$: combined $= 14 > 10$. No deviation. ✅
  - $\{1,4\}$: combined $= 13 > 8$. No deviation. ✅
  - $\{2,3\}$: combined $= 7 < 9$. **DEVIATION!** ⚠️
  - $\{2,4\}$: combined $= 6 < 7$. **DEVIATION!** ⚠️
  - $\{3,4\}$: combined $= 5 < 6$. **DEVIATION!** ⚠️

> **Larsen Stability FAILS!** Players 2, 3, 4 are receiving *exactly* their solo payoff, while the marginal surplus $v(S) - \sum v(\{i\})$ for their coalitions is **distributed entirely to Player 1**.

**Step 4: Refine the Model using CR Constraints**

To be Larsen-stable, we need:
- $x_2 + x_3 \geq 9$
- $x_2 + x_4 \geq 7$
- $x_3 + x_4 \geq 6$

Player 1 maximizes $x_1 = 20 - x_2 - x_3 - x_4$ subject to:
- $x_2 \geq 4, x_3 \geq 3, x_4 \geq 2$
- $x_2 + x_3 \geq 9$
- $x_2 + x_4 \geq 7$
- $x_3 + x_4 \geq 6$

**Solve the Linear Program:**

To minimize $x_2 + x_3 + x_4$, we try to set each pair sum to its lower bound.

Adding the three pair constraints:
$$(x_2 + x_3) + (x_2 + x_4) + (x_3 + x_4) \geq 9 + 7 + 6 = 22$$
$$2(x_2 + x_3 + x_4) \geq 22 \implies x_2 + x_3 + x_4 \geq 11$$

So the minimum is achieved when **all three pair constraints are tight simultaneously**. Solving:
- $x_2 + x_3 = 9$
- $x_2 + x_4 = 7$
- $x_3 + x_4 = 6$

Adding all three: $2(x_2 + x_3 + x_4) = 22 \implies x_2 + x_3 + x_4 = 11$.

From the equations:
- $(x_2 + x_3) - (x_2 + x_4) = 9 - 7 = 2 \implies x_3 - x_4 = 2$
- $(x_2 + x_4) - (x_3 + x_4) = 7 - 6 = 1 \implies x_2 - x_3 = 1$

So $x_4 = x_3 - 2$, $x_2 = x_3 + 1$, and $x_2 + x_3 + x_4 = (x_3 + 1) + x_3 + (x_3 - 2) = 3x_3 - 1 = 11 \implies x_3 = 4$.

Thus: $x_3 = 4, x_2 = 5, x_4 = 2$, and $x_1 = 20 - 11 = 9$.

**Refined Larsen-Stable Outcome:** $\mathbf{x}^{\text{stable}} = (9, 5, 4, 2)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reconciliation:** The first SPE computation (Player 1 maximizes unilaterally) does NOT yield a Larsen-stable outcome. The **Larsen-stable refinement** requires Player 1 to share *just enough* surplus with each potential deviating pair to make deviation unprofitable. This yields $(9, 5, 4, 2)$ — a more equitable and stability-consistent prediction.

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Comparison of Larsen Model with Other Solution Concepts**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Actual-Style Paper]**

A 3-player TU-game has characteristic function:
$$v(\{1\}) = 6, \ v(\{2\}) = 4, \ v(\{3\}) = 3$$
$$v(\{1,2\}) = 12, \ v(\{1,3\}) = 10, \ v(\{2,3\}) = 8, \ v(\{1,2,3\}) = 16$$

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compute the **Shapley value** for this game. Show all steps.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Apply the **Larsen Model** with Player 2 as the designated proposer. Determine the **Larsen-stable imputation** and compare it with the Shapley value. Comment on the **first-mover advantage** effect.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) Solution — Shapley Value Computation:**

For $n = 3$, the Shapley weights for player $i$ over all orderings are $\frac{1}{6}$ each, but we group by coalition size.

**For Player 1:**

$$\phi_1(v) = \frac{1}{3}[v(\{1\}) - v(\emptyset)] + \frac{1}{6}[v(\{1,2\}) - v(\{2\})] + \frac{1}{6}[v(\{1,3\}) - v(\{3\})] + \frac{1}{3}[v(\{1,2,3\}) - v(\{2,3\})]$$

$$= \frac{1}{3}(6) + \frac{1}{6}(12 - 4) + \frac{1}{6}(10 - 3) + \frac{1}{3}(16 - 8)$$

$$= 2 + \frac{8}{6} + \frac{7}{6} + \frac{8}{3} = 2 + \frac{4}{3} + \frac{7}{6} + \frac{8}{3}$$

Converting to sixths: $\frac{12}{6} + \frac{8}{6} + \frac{7}{6} + \frac{16}{6} = \frac{43}{6} \approx 7.167$

**For Player 2:**

$$\phi_2(v) = \frac{1}{3}(4) + \frac{1}{6}(12 - 6) + \frac{1}{6}(8 - 3) + \frac{1}{3}(16 - 10)$$

$$= \frac{4}{3} + 1 + \frac{5}{6} + 2 = \frac{8}{6} + \frac{6}{6} + \frac{5}{6} + \frac{12}{6} = \frac{31}{6} \approx 5.167$$

**For Player 3:**

$$\phi_3(v) = \frac{1}{3}(3) + \frac{1}{6}(10 - 6) + \frac{1}{6}(8 - 4) + \frac{1}{3}(16 - 12)$$

$$= 1 + \frac{2}{3} + \frac{2}{3} + \frac{4}{3} = \frac{6 + 4 + 4 + 8}{6} = \frac{22}{6} = \frac{11}{3} \approx 3.667$$

**Verification:** $\frac{43 + 31 + 22}{6} = \frac{96}{6} = 16$ ✓

**Shapley Value:** $\phi(v) = \left(\frac{43}{6}, \frac{31}{6}, \frac{11}{3}\right) \approx (7.167, 5.167, 3.667)$.

**Part (b) Solution — Larsen Model with Player 2 as Proposer:**

**Step 1:** The extensive-form game:
- Player 2 proposes $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, x_3)$ with $x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 16$.
- Players 1 and 3 accept or reject.
- Fallback: $v(\{1\}) = 6$, $v(\{3\}) = 3$ (Player 2's fallback is $v(\{2\}) = 4$ if she were to be rejected, but as proposer her own payoff is at her discretion).

**Step 2:** Backward induction — Players 1 and 3 accept if $x_i \geq v(\{i\})$.
- $x_1 \geq 6$
- $x_3 \geq 3$

Player 2 maximizes $x_2 = 16 - x_1 - x_3$, subject to $x_1 \geq 6, x_3 \geq 3$.

**SPE offer:** $x_1 = 6, x_3 = 3, x_2 = 7$.

**Step 3: Verify Larsen Stability:**

- $x_1 + x_2 = 13 > v(\{1,2\}) = 12$ ✅
- $x_1 + x_3 = 9 < v(\{1,3\}) = 10$ ⚠️
- $x_2 + x_3 = 10 > v(\{2,3\}) = 8$ ✅

**Pairwise deviation $\{1,3\}$ profits!** Players 1 and 3 together can do strictly better.

**Step 4: Refine using CR:**

We need $x_1 + x_3 \geq 10$. Player 2 minimizes $x_1 + x_3$ subject to $x_1 \geq 6, x_3 \geq 3, x_1 + x_3 \geq 10$. The minimum is $x_1 + x_3 = 10$, with $x_1 = 6, x_3 = 4$ (or any distribution with $x_1 \geq 6, x_3 \geq 4$).

For maximum fairness (or by convention, allocate the surplus to the more "constrained" player), we set $x_1 = 6, x_3 = 4, x_2 = 16 - 10 = 6$.

**Larsen-Stable Imputation:** $\mathbf{x}^{\text{Larsen}} = (6, 6, 4)$.

**Step 5: Comparison Table**

| Player | Shapley Value | Larsen SPE | Difference |
|--------|---------------|------------|------------|
| 1 | 7.167 | 6.000 | $-1.167$ |
| 2 | 5.167 | 6.000 | $+0.833$ |
| 3 | 3.667 | 4.000 | $+0.333$ |
| **Sum** | **16.000** | **16.000** | **0.000** |

**Step 6: Comment on First-Mover Advantage:**

> The **Larsen Model with Player 2 as proposer** gives Player 2 a payoff of $6$, compared to her Shapley value of $5.167$. This is a **$\sim$16% increase** attributable entirely to the **first-mover advantage** of being the designated proposer. Player 1, who is the highest-value player in the Shapley sense, actually *loses* $1.167$ units under the Larsen prediction because she must yield some of her expected surplus to the proposer. This is the classic **"proposer's premium"** in bargaining theory: whoever has the right to make the first offer captures a disproportionate share of the surplus, even though all players are equally rational.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Larsen Model Questions:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the Backward Induction Step (2–3 marks lost):** Many students jump directly to the imputation without explicitly writing the offer-acceptance game tree. *Always draw the game tree, even briefly.*
>
> 2. **Forgetting Larsen Stability Verification (3–4 marks lost):** Just because the SPE offer is computed does not mean the outcome is Larsen-stable. You MUST check all pairwise (and larger) deviations against the CR constraints.
>
> 3. **Confusing Larsen SPE with Naive SPE (1–2 marks lost):** The *first-stage* SPE (where proposer offers only the fallback payoffs) is **NOT** the Larsen-stable outcome. The Larsen Model requires the **refinement** where pair-wise coalition deviations are also blocked.
>
> 4. **Computational Errors in Shapley Value (1–2 marks lost):** When computing $\phi_i(v)$, double-check the weights $\frac{|S|!(n-|S|-1)!}{n!}$ and the marginal contributions $v(S \cup \{i\}) - v(S)$. The verification $\sum_i \phi_i = v(N)$ is your safety check.
>
> 5. **Mis-stating the Imputation Conditions (1 mark lost):** An imputation requires BOTH Individual Rationality AND Group Rationality. Do not omit either.
>
> 6. **Hand-waving the Comparison Step (1–2 marks lost):** When comparing Larsen SPE with the Shapley value, always provide a *quantitative* table and a *qualitative* interpretation. The KTU examiner awards 2 marks for a thoughtful comparison.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Larsen Model**

- ✅ The **Larsen Model** combines **coalitional game theory** with a **non-cooperative extensive-form offer-and-acceptance game**.
- ✅ The solution concept is **Subgame Perfect Equilibrium (SPE)** computed via **backward induction**.
- ✅ A **Larsen-stable outcome** must satisfy: **(i) IR**, **(ii) CR for all pairs and grand coalition**, **(iii) No profitable deviation** by any coalition.
- ✅ The **proposer captures a first-mover premium** — the first player to offer typically receives a larger share than under axiomatic solutions like the Shapley value.
- ✅ The Larsen Model **always produces a prediction** (unlike the Core, which can be empty).
- ✅ Key formulas to memorize:
  - **Shapley:** $\phi_i(v) = \sum_{S \subseteq N \setminus \{i\}} \frac{|S|! (n-|S|-1)!}{n!} [v(S \cup \{i\}) - v(S)]$
  - **Excess:** $e(S, \mathbf{x}) = v(S) - \sum_{i \in S} x_i$
  - **Nucleolus:** $\mathbf{x}^{\text{nuc}} = \arg\min \text{lex} \max_S e(S, \mathbf{x})$
  - **Rubinstein 2-player:** $(x_1, x_2) = \left(\frac{1}{1+\delta}, \frac{\delta}{1+\delta}\right)$
- ✅ Applications: **Spectrum auctions, smart grids, cloud computing, supply chain alliances, blockchain validator sets**.
- ✅ For $n = 3$ problems, **always draw the game tree** before computing the SPE — this is what differentiates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.
- ✅ The Larsen Model is **not superadditive-requiring** — it works for *any* TU-game, making it strictly more general than core-based approaches.
- ✅ When the **Core is non-empty**, the Larsen stable outcome lies within the Core; when the **Core is empty**, the Larsen Model still gives a meaningful prediction.
- ✅ In any Larsen problem, **always verify stability** by computing all pair sums and comparing to $v(\{i,j\})$.
- ✅ The Larsen Model is part of **Module 4: Mechanism Design and Coalitional Games** in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECST753.
- ✅ For deeper study, the model connects to **VCG mechanisms**, **stable matching** (Gale-Shapley), and **matching markets** — expect cross-module questions in the KTU End-Semester Exam.
